The\n\nsections need no change.\n\n26_16_ In thc case of speci\ufb01c bequests, certain questions arise where the sections 159 and\ngoods are described with reference to a certain place. The Connection of the 161\ngoods with that place might have disappeared at the time of death, and the\ncourt has then to decide whether the connection is treated _as to be material so\nthat me speci\ufb01c bequest is adeemed, or whether the connection is, in the circum-\nstances of the case, to be disregarded. To deal with this problem, two general\n\n&#x27;Section 153. .\n&#x27;Section 151 to be omitted, if the above recommendation 13 accepted.\n\n.The assumption is that section 151 shall be deleted.\n\n\n\n[60\n\nrules have been enacted, in section 160. The first is that if the removal of the\ngoods from the speci\ufb01c place is due to a temporary cause, or by fraud, or\nwithout the knowledge or sanction of the testator&#x27;. then there is no ademption.\nThis is to be found in section 160.\n\nSecondly, section 161 provides that removal of the thing from the speci\ufb01ed\nplace does not constitute an ademption, where the place is referred to only in\norder to complete the description of what the testator meant to bequeath. The\nillustrations to the section explain its purport. But the third illustration seems\nto stretch the language of the section. It puts the facts as under :\n\n&quot;(iii) A bequeaths to B all his goods on board a certain ship, then lying\nin the river Hoogli. The goods are removed by A&#x27;s direction to a ware-\nhouse, in which they remain at the time of As death.&quot;\n\nThe illustration then provides that the legacy is not revoked by ademption\n\nRacommcndatgonm 26.17. Now, although it could be said that here the mention of the place\namend section 161. was, in a sense, necessary to complete the description of what the testator meant\nto bequeath, a more convincing reason for not allowing ademption in such a\ncase is that, by the very nature of the case, the situation mentioned or\nthe place mentioned could only be temporary. A more accurate way of expressing\nsection, therefore, to cover such a case, would be to add, at the end of\nsection 161, some such words as &quot;or where, by its very nature, the place is such\nthat the thing lItt&#x27;(]lI\u00a3&#x27;(ll/I(&#x27;(l &#x27;.t&#x27;0ul&#x27;t1 I&#x27;(&#x27;/)1./llll at that place only for u tt&gt;mp0rar_\\&#x27;\nperiod.&quot; a\n\nWe recommend that section 161 should be amended as above.\n\nV. Receipt by testator\n\n26.18. Section 162 provides that where the &quot;thing bequeathed&quot;-----which really\nmeans &quot;the thing speci\ufb01cally bequeathed&quot;,------is not the right to receive something\nof value from a third person, but the money or other commodity which may\nbe received from the third person by the testator himself or by his representatives,\nthe receipt of such sum of money or other commodity by the testator shall not\nconstitute an ademption; but if he mixes it up with the general mass of his pro--\nperty, the legacy is adecme-d. This section is to be contrasted with section 154,\nwhereunder&quot; there is ademption where the thing speci\ufb01cally bequeathed is the\nright to receive something of value from the third party, and the testator himself\nreceives it. The reason why section 154 provides for ademption is that the\nthing bequeathed is itself the subject to be extinguished, while the reason why\nsection 162 prohibits ademption is that the amount received retains its separate\nidentity. 1 3 *?;~4&#x27;&quot;\u00a7&#x27;3{\n\nIn practice, it may not always be easy to determine, on the language of the\nwill, whether the case falls under section 154 or section 162. In the absence\nof concrete Indian cases raising questions, however, it is not considered to disturb\nthe two sections on this point.\n\nSection 162.\n\nRecommendmion 26.19. However. it should be made clear that section 162 applies only\n\nto amend section to &quot;speci\ufb01c bequests&quot;, since the expression &quot;thing bequeathed&quot;, in this context&#x27;\n\n152- means the thing speci\ufb01cally bequeathed. This is clear from the later reference to\n&quot;mixture with the general mass&quot;. We recommend that section 162 should be\namended for the purpose, by substituting, for the words &quot;the thing bequeath &quot;,\n\nthe words &quot;the thing .vpect&#x27;ficall_v bequeatlted.&quot;\n\nVi. Change in the thing bequeathed by operation of law.\n\n26.20. Under section 163, where a thing speci\ufb01cally bequeathed undergoes\na change between the date of the will and the testator&#x27;s death, and the change\ntakes place by operation of law, or in the course of execution of the provisions\nof any legal instrument under which the thing bequeathed was held, the legacy\nis not adeemed by reason of such change.\n\nSection 163-\nin a thing speci\ufb01-\ncally bequeathed.\n\n*LTo:npa.&#x27;.: Section 154.\n&#x27;Para 26.8.. supra.\n\n\n\nI61\n\n26.21. It should be noted that this is not the rule in England, even though Position\nsome of the commentators in India state that the Indian section is based upon Bhmdv\n\nthe English law. In England, ademption takes place even in such circumstances,\nunless the statute which brought about the change in the nature of the pro-\nperty makes a contrary provision&#x27;. The situation has been described as one of\n&quot;statutory disappropriation of property rights in favour of the state?&quot; Often,\nhowever, statutes do make a provision preventing ademption.\n\nin En-\n\n26.22. However, the legislature in India has adopted a different rule, per-Ru], in India,\n\nhaps on the assumption that since the change in question takes place without\nthe will of the testator, one can presume that he had not changed his intentions\nas to affect the destination of the subject matter.\n\n26.23. At this stage we should refer to a Delhi case relevant to change by new\n\noperation of law. A Hindu executed a will according to which, on his death,\nhis property was to go to the second wife for her life time and, after the death,\nequally to his son&#x27;s sons from the first wife and the sons from the second wife.\nThe property was in the N. W. Province (now in Pakistan). After the partition\nthe family moved to India. On his death the widow claimed and obtained\ncompensation for the property left in Pakistan on the basis pf the will. She\nlater purchased some property in India from out of the compensation so received.\nOn the death of the widow, the son&#x27;s sons from the \ufb01rst wife claimed half\nshare in the property so purchased out of the compensation, relying on the will&#x27;.\n\nIt was held that the claimants were not entitled to the half share, The\ncompensation received was in thetform of money. By no stretch of imagination\nit could be treated as property &quot;situated in Pakistan&quot;. Once the property had\nceased to exist in its original form, the limitation on the rights of enjoyment no\nlonger applied. It would be too much to say that this compensation money had\nto be preserved for ever in whatever form it goes and treated as if it was the\nproperty referred to in the will. The widow could not be treated as being a\ntrustee under the terms of the will. She had no right to sell at all, being a limited\nowner, If by accident, the property is sold either through legislation or through\nother circumstances, then there is no obligation on her to live on the income\nof that compensation and leave the corpus to the persons entitled to the\nresidue of the estate. Therefore, the doctrine of conversion could not be invoked\nin the present case.\n\n26.24. The High Court further held that there was no direction in the\nwill that any compensation received in respect of that property would go to the\npersons who would otherwise have got the property. This meant that the gift\nhad been adeemed&#x27;, because it cannot be given effect to. It was observed that\nthis was unfortunate, because if this situation had at all been in the mind of\nthe testator, he could have made a direction regarding the same. The testator\ncould have made a codicil or a new will regarding the compensation. But the\nwill was silent regarding the compensation and it could not be made operative\nqua the compensation. In the circumstances, the suit was dismissed,\n\n26.25. The judgment does not discuss section 163, and attention of the\ncourt was perhaps not drawn to the section.\n\n26.26. We may discuss another aspect of section 163. Where a change in Chang:\nthe thing bequeathed takes place by an order of the court, it should apparently Vofcourt.\n\nbe regarded as a change by &#x27;operation of law&#x27; within section 163. Here again,\nthe common law was different, as illustrated by English cases&#x27; where, by an\norder in lunacy proceedings, certain shares speci\ufb01cally bequeathed were directed\n\n&#x27;Mellows, Law of Succession (1977), pages 523, 524.\n&#x27;Sea; Re-Viscount Galway&#x27;s Will, (1950) Ch. 1; (1950) 2 All ER. 419.\n&#x27;Chaman Lal v. Kundan Lal, A.I.R. 1979 Delhi 240, parasll tol4(D.K.KapurJ.)(Novcm-\nber-December).\n&#x27;Halsbury, 3rd Ed., Vol. 39 p. 934, Article 1412, cited.\n&#x27;(al Jones v. Green, Law Reports 5 Eq. 555.\n(b) Re-Freer, 22 Chancery Division 622.\n\nC3.S\u20ac.\n\nby order\n\n\n\n-.--5.\u00bb,-. .\n\n162\n\nto be sold, and the proceeds were to be invested in other securities. The bequest\nof such shares was held to be adeemed. The position in England was later\naltered by statute&#x27;.\n\n%c;1c;irpgr;wimoll?4- Ci\ufb01c 12l6.2b7. ling Igkescps to SCCIIOIIII 164, which provides that where a thing spe-\nknowledge of [mm a y lelquea ie un ergocs a c angc without a ltnowledge or sanction of the\nton testator, t ere is no adcmption. This situation is analogous to that under section\n. 160, whercunder removal of the goods from the place described in the will with-\nout knowledge or sanction of the testator does not lead to ademption. No change\n\nis required in this section.\n\nSection 165-Stock <a href=\"/doc/1160274/\" id=\"a_663\">26</a>.28. Section 165 provides that where stock which has been speci\ufb01cally\n\nreplaced after loan-bequeathed is lent to a third party on condition that it shall be replaced, and\nit is replaced accordingly, the legacy is not adeemed, This section also does not\nseem to require any charige,\n\nsection 166, <a href=\"/doc/1160274/\" id=\"a_664\">26</a>.29. Section 166 reads as follows :\n\n&quot;I66. Where stock speci\ufb01cally bequeathed is sold, and an equal quantity\nof the same stock is afterwards purchased and belongs to the testator at\nhis death. the legacy is not adeemed&quot;.\n\nThis section is based on the &#x27;dictum of Lord Talbot in an early case&#x27;-&quot;.\nLord Talbot said in that case : &quot;If the selling out the stock is an evidence to\npresume an alteration of the intention of the testator, surely his buying in again\nis as _strong an evidence of his intention that the legatee should have it again&quot;.\nIt should be noted, however, that later it has been held&#x27; that in England a legacy\nis adecmed by the sale of the stock. and will not he reviverl by :1 new purchase\n0f_SlIllll21l&#x27; stock by the testator.\n\nThere is, perhaps, some thing to be said for both the views. It can, on\nthe one hand, be said that the testator would not have intended to remove a\nbene\ufb01t already conferred. On the other hand, it can be said that he would not\nhave sold the stock if he wished to continue the bene\ufb01t. In this position, it is\nbetter not to change the section, leaving it to the testator to make an express\nmodi\ufb01cation of his will, if he so desires.\n\nCHAPTER 27\nPAY.VIF.l\\&quot;l&#x27; OF LIABILITIES IN RESPECT OF THE SUBJECT OF A\nBEQUEST: SECTION 167 T0 170\n\nScope. 27.1. The principle is that in case of bequests of an immovable property,\nthe rents and taxes in respect of that property upto the time of the testator&#x27;:\ndeath, should be paid out of the testator&#x27;s estate. and the taxes etc. after the\ndeath of the testator should be paid by the legatee. These sections are silent as to tax on income. As regards the liability\nof the legal representative to pay income~tax, the law is that where tax has\nalready been assessed as payable by a person and he dies, his executor, adminis-\ntrator or other legal representative is liable to pay the same out of his estate so\nfar as the estate is capable of meeting the charge of income-tax&#x27;.\n\n<a href=\"/doc/789969/\" id=\"a_668\">The Income Tax Act</a>&quot; casts a liability upon the executor or administrator\nto pay the tax which the Icstazor would have been liable to pay. This obviously\nrelates to the liability relative to the period before death. As section 169 has not\nso far created any problems, we would leave the section as it is,\n\n27.7. Section 170 deals with liability in respect of call or other payment on\nstock in a joint stock company, and is as follows :---\n\n&quot;In the absence of any direction in the will where there is a speci\ufb01c bequest\nof stock in a joint stock company, if any call or other payment is due from\nthe testator at the time of his death, in respect of the stock, such call or pay-\nment shall, as between the testators estate and the legatees be borne by the\nestate; but, if any call or other payment; becomes due in respect of such\nstock after the tesLator&#x27;s death, the same shall,  between the testator&#x27;s\nestate and the legatee, be borne by the legatce, if he accepts the bequest.&quot;\nThere are live illustrations to the section, as follows 2--\n\n&quot;(i) A bequeaths to B his shares in a certain railway. At A&#x27;s death\nthere was due from him the sum of 100 rupees in respect of each\nshare. being the amount of 3 call which had been duly inadc, and the\nsum of five rupees in respect of each share, being the amount of in.-\nterest which had accrued due in respect of the call. These payments\nmust be bore by A&#x27;s estate.\n\n(ii) A has agreed to take 50 shares in an intended joint stock company,\nand has contracted to pay up 100 rupees in respect of each share,\nwhich sum must be paid before his title to the shares can be completed.\nA bequeaths these shares to B. The estate of A must make good the\npayments which were necessary to complete A&#x27;s title.\n\n(_iii)_ A bequeaths to B his shares in a certain railway. B accepts the\nlegacy. After A&#x27;: death, 2. call is made in respect of the shares. B must\npay the call.\n\n(iv) A bequeaths to B his shares in a joint stock company. B accepts\nthe bequest. Afterwards the affairs of the company are wound up, and\neach share--holder is called upon for contribution. The amount of the\ncontribution must be borne by the legatee.\n\n&quot;(v) A is the owner of ten shares in a railway company. At a meeting\nheld during his lifetime a call is made of \ufb01fty rupees per share, pay-\nable by three instalments. A bequeaths his shares to B and dies bet-\nween the day \ufb01xed for the payment of the first and the day \ufb01xed\nfor the payment of the second instalment, and without having paid the\nfirst instalment. A&#x27;s estate must pay the \ufb01rst instalment, and B, if he\naccepts the legacy, must pay the remaining instalments&quot;.\n\n27.8. The terminology of section 170 seems to suffer from a serious di\ufb01cieney,\ninasmuch as it uses the words &quot;stock i.n a joint stock company&quot;, and omits to\nmention &#x27;shares&#x27;. though illustrations (ii) to (iv) to the section do mention\nshares. To an ordinary person, the word &#x27;stock would not include &#x27;shares&#x27;. Prima\nfacie, it appears desirable that shares should be continued speci\ufb01cally in the section.\n\nThe legislative usage in the Companies Ac&#x27; may be referred to in this\ncontext. Under that Act3, &#x27;stock&#x27; is the aggregate of fully paid up shares legally\nconsolidated, portions of which agreogate may be transferred to split up intg\nfractions of any amount of shares. &#x27;Share&#x27; means share in the share capital of\n\n&#x27;Section 159, Income Taxt 1961.\n&#x27;<a href=\"/doc/144582/\" id=\"a_669\">Section 159</a>, Income Tax Act, 1961.\n&#x27;<a href=\"/doc/71233/\" id=\"a_670\">Section 566</a>, Companies Act, 1956.\n\n\n\n165\n\nthe company and includes stock, except when a distinction between stock and\nshare is expressed. Where the shares itrtve been fully paid up, they may be turned\ninto stock, and notice must be given to the Registrar.\n\nTl1e term &#x27;Joint Stock Company&#x27; is de\ufb01ned by the <a href=\"/doc/1353758/\" id=\"a_671\">Companies Act</a>&#x27;, as it\ncompany having a permanent paid up or a nominal share capital of a \ufb01xed\namount divided into shares. also of \ufb01xed amount, or held and transferable as stock,\nor divided and held partly in the one way and partly in the other, and\nformed on the principle of having, for its members the holders of those shares\nor that stock. and no other person.\n\n27.9. Reference may also be made in this connection to a decision of the\nHouse of Lords&#x27;. In that case, the testator had bequeathed to his wife &#x27;Ieasehold\nestates and all such stocks in the public funds, or shares in any Railway of which\n1 may die possessed,&#x27; and, on failure of issue, he gave to his brother the same\nleashold estate, stock. shares, moneys and securities&quot;. The question was whether\nthe &#x27;shares&#x27; and &#x27;stocks&#x27; were two difrerent things, in view of the fact that the\ntestator consciously used both these terms in his will. It was held that manifestly,\nfor every purpose, share and stocks were alike share when paid up, grew into\nstocl&lt;,..and. were put upon a special Register for the convenient management of\nbusiness, but, in substance, they continued to be the same thing, and may well\npass by the same words unless there be a clear difference made between them.\n\n27.10. It appears to us that instead of driving the reader to deriving the\nmeaning of &#x27;stock&#x27; from the illustrations under section 170 or to other sources,\nthe Act should expressly mention the word &#x27;shares&#x27; in the body of the main\nsection. The layman is more frequently concerned with &#x27;share&#x27; than with stock.\nWe, therefore, recommend that section 170 should be suitably amended, so as to\nadd an express mention of &#x27;shares&#x27;. The following is :1 suggested re--draft of the\n\nsection for the purpose :\nRevised section 170\n\n&quot;170. In the absence of any direction in the will, where there is a speci\ufb01c\nbequest of stock in a joint stock company or shares in a company with\nlimited liabilities,\n\n(a) it&quot; any call or oher payment is due from the testator at the time\nhis death in respect of the stock or shares, such call or payment shall, as\nbetween the testator&#x27;s estate and the legatee, be borne by the estate;\n\nbut-\n\n(b) if any call or other payment becomes due in respect of such stock\nor shares after the testator&#x27;s death, the same shall, as between the testator&#x27;s\nestate and the legatee, be borne by the legatee, if he accepts the bequest&quot;.\n\nIllustrations as at present.\n\nCHAPTER 28\nBEQUESTS OF THINGS\n\n28.1. Legacies to creditors and portioners form the subject matter of\nsection 177 to 179. Before discussing the gist of sections 177 to 179, it would\nbe worthwhile to point out that the provisions of these sections depart from the\nEnglish law, and that the departure is deliberate and justified, The rule in\nEngland is that where a bequest is given to a person who is a creditor of the\n\ntestator, then, in the absence of an intention to the contrary, the bequest is_\n\nregarded as given in &quot;satisfaction&quot; of the prc-existing debt, that is to say, it is\nregarded as an extinguishment of the existing claim, and not as a separate legacy.\nThis rule of equity is known as &quot;satisfaction of debts by legacies&quot;. Similarly, in\nEngland, where a &quot;portion&quot; or part of a person&#x27;s estate agreed to be given\nto a child is followed by a legacy to that child. the rule that equity leans ag_ainst\ndouble portions is attracted. In the absence of an intention to the contrary, the\npresumption of equity is that the bequest is in satisfaction of the portion agreed\nto be given to the child. The presumption is based on the improbability of a parent\nintending a double portion for one child to the prejudice of other children\n\n\n\n169\n\n30.2. To make the discussion comprehensive, it should be pointed out Rule of_&quot;Satis- _\nthat in England, the equitable rule of &quot;satisfaction&quot;, considered in all its as- f&quot;\u00b0&quot;\u00b0&quot;&quot;&#x27;&quot;E&quot;3&#x27;&quot;&#x27;\npacts, arises&#x27; in four classes of cases, namely, (i) satisfaction of debts by lega-\ncies&#x27;, (ii) satisfaction of legacies by subsequent legacies, (iii) satisfaction of\nportiops by legacies&#x27;, and (iv) satisfaction of legacies by portions or advance-\nments.\n\nIn India, <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_673\">sections 177</a> to <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_674\">179</a> of the Act make a clean sweep of the doctrine\nof &quot;satisfaction&quot; in regard to all the four cases mentioned above. The first case\nis dealt with in section 177; the second case is dealt with in section 179;\n\nThe third case is dealt with in section 178, and the fourth case is dealt\nwith in section 179. vide the words &quot;by settlement or otherwise&#x27; which occur\nin that section.\n\n30.3. This departure from English law was deliberate, as is evident from Dcpanurefromsm\nthe Report of the Law Commission&#x27;. The Law Commissioners regarded the ject law dclibetat:\nEnglish rule as objectionable and inapplicable to India, and wished to avoid the\ndi\ufb02icult enquiry as to the intentions of the testator which has to be undertaken\naccording to the English ruie. .\n\nWe are now in :1 position to discuss the section proper.\n\n30.4. This takes us to section 177. Sect&#x27;ons 177 and 178 may be considered section; 177 an\ntogether. They read-- 173-\n\n&quot;l77. Where a debtor bequeaths a legacy to his creditor, and it does not\nappear from the will that the legacy is meant as a satisfaction of the debt,\nthe creditor shall be entitled to the legacy, as well as to amount of the\ndebt.&quot;\n\n178. Where a parent, who is under obligation by contract to provide a por-\ntion for a child. fails to do so, and afterwards bequeaths a legacy to the\nchild, and does not intimate by his will that the legacy is meant as a satis-\nfaction of the portion, the child shall be entitled to receive the legacy, as\nwell as the portion.\n\nILLUSTRATION\n\nA. by articles entered into in contemplation of his marriage with B, covenant-\ned that he would pay to each of the daughters of the intended marriage a\nportion of 20,000 rupees on her marriage. This covenant having been\nbroken, A bequeaths 20,000 rupees to each of the married daughters of\nhimself and B. The legatees are entitled to the benefit of this bequest in\naddition to their portions.&quot;\n\n30.5. The sections, so far as they go, are satisfactory and judicial decisions Sections I77&amp; 17.\ndo not seem to have created any serious problems. There is one situation, how- Need for speci\ufb01\never, about which a speci\ufb01c rule appears to be desirable. There is no provision rule as to bequest t:\nin the Act as regards bequest to a debtor. though a bequest by a debtor is d\u00b0b&#x27;\u00b0&#x27;-\ncovered by section 177. It appears that in England. where the legatee of a gene-\nral legacy of a share or a residue is a debtor to the estate, he is not entitled\nto receive the legacy without first bringing his debt into account; and, for\nthis purpose, it makes no difference whether the debt is alive or is barred by limi-\ntation. A similar rule has been judiciallyapplied in India. In a Calcutta, case&#x27;,\n\nA and B were indebted to the estate, and died without .sari.rfying their debt (the\n\n1Pomero_\\&#x27;, Equity Jurisrvudence, paragraph 521, referred to in ND. Basu, Law of Succession\n(1957), pages 551,552.\n\n*TrIIbnt \\&#x27;. .5&#x27;/u&#x27;rwrb1u&#x27;y. (1711) Pr. Ch. 39-1.\n\n&quot;Exp. P_rv., IS \\&#x27;cs 140.\n\n&#x27;Re pollork, 23 Ch. D. 552.\n\n&quot;Report of the Law Cwnznission, 23rd June, 1363, Ga7ette of Iridia\u00bb---Ist July, 1864, page 54.\n&#x27;Loke Nam Mulliek V.Gdoyachwar, (1881) l.I_.R. 7 Cal. 644.\n\n\n\npcommcndation.\n\n&#x27;pope.\n\nI\n\ntion l80-~Cjr-\ninstances in which\ntion takes place.\n\ntxcine of appro-\n; te and reprobate.\n\n170\n\ndebts were barred by limitation). Their descendants claimed a share in the estate\n\nof the testator, apparently under it will. It was held that they could not be allowed&#x27;\n\nto receive the share without iirst stllls&#x27;l}ing the debts due from them to the\nestate. .\n\nThis is really analogous to setting off the debt against the legacy: Justice\nand the presumed intention of the testator support such a course.\n\n30.6. It may be w0rth\\\\&#x27;hile to codify this judicial interpretation. We re-\ncommend that for this purpose :1 new section l77A should be inserted as\nfollows :--\n\n&quot;l77A. Where a ccrditor bcqueatlzs a legacy to /as debtor, and it does\nnot appear from the will that the legacy is to be paid even if the debt is\nnot repaid by the dcbtor, the debtor shall not be entitled to the legacy unless\nso much of the debt as has /tcrotm&#x27; (ll/(&#x27; and payable has been repaid.&quot;\n\nCHAPTER 31\nELECTION; SECTIONS 180 T0 190\nI. Principle of election\n31.1. The topic of election is dealth with in eleven sections (180 to 190).\n\nA person on whom a benefit is conferred by a will must adopt the whole\ncontents of the instrument, confomi to all its provisions and renounce every right\ninconsistent with it. This in brief, is the gist of the doctrine of election which\nforms the basis of this Chapter. The basic section is section 180, the rest being\nconcerned with particular applications of the doctrine or quali\ufb01cations thereof.\n\n31.2. Section 180 formulates the principle in these terms.\n\n&quot;I80. Where a person, by his will, professes to dispose of something which\nhe has no right to dispose of, the person to whom the thing belongs shall\nelect either to con\ufb01rm such disposition or to dissent from it, and, in the\nlatter case, he shall give up any bene\ufb01ts it-/zirlz may have been provided for\nhim by the will&quot;&#x27;.\n\n31.3. In Douglas Benzies v. Upphelby&#x27;, Lord Robertson referred to the\nprinciple of election as being the same as the doctrine&quot; of &quot;approbate and repro-\nl~:1tc&quot;,A and observed :\n\n&quot;In considering the merits of the decision appealed against, it is well to\nremember what is the doctrine of approbate and reprobate invoked by the\nappellant. Although the name is different, the principle as was laid down\nby Lord Eldon in Ker v. Wauchope&#x27;, is the same as that of the English law\nof election. It is against equity that any one should take against a man&#x27;s\nwill and also under it. This rests on no arti\ufb01cial rule, but on plain fair\ndealing. If any one has the right by law to take a share of a testator&#x27;s\nestate, which the testator has not given but has otherwise disposed of, that\nperson takes it against the will and cannot go on to found on the will and\nclaim its bene\ufb01ts&quot;.\n\nIn a later case&quot; Lord Atkinson quoted with approval the passage above cited\nfrom the judgment of Lord Robertson.\n\n&#x27;Emphasis adopted.\n\n&#x27;DougIas Menzies v.Upp/telby, (1908) A.(&#x27;. 224. 232.\n,Scc also para 3l .2. supra\n\n&#x27;Ker v. Wauchope, (1819) 1 Bligh l.\n\n&quot;Pt&#x27;rntan v. Gram Ewing, (1911) A.C. 217.\n\n\n\n1/]\n\n31.4. The principle that 21 person cannot both &quot;approbate and reprobate&quot;\n( to use terms used in Scottish Law)&#x27;, \ufb01nds expression in the law of Wills&quot;\nas also in the law of deeds&quot;. The principle is discussed by the Editor of Swaiistoifs\nReports. in a very learned note on the leading English east; on the subject&#x27;.\n\n3l.5&#x27;. The principle is of universal application. and is not peculiar to Eng- Um&#x27;\\,\u00a2,-sat\nlish law. As the Privy Council observed long ago&#x27;, it is &quot;common to all law application.\nwhich is based on the rules of justice. vi7.., the principle that 11 party shall\nnot at the same time n\ufb02irni and disa\ufb02inn the same transaCtion~--atiirm it as far\nas it is for his bene\ufb01t and disaf\ufb01rm it. as far as it is to his prejudice&quot;.\n\nIt&#x27;. therefore. it tcstator has purported to dispose of property which is not\nhis own. and has given a benefit to the person to whom that property belongs.\nthe dcvisec or legzitcc, acccp1r&#x27;irg the benefit so given to him. must make good the\ntestatofs attempted disposition. But. if on th-e contrary, that person choses to\nenforce his proprietary rights against the testator&#x27;s disposition. then equity will\nsequester the property given to him. for the purpose of making .tatz&#x27;sfacn&#x27;on out\nof it to the person whom he has disappointed by the assertion of those rights.\nThis is the broad position in l&#x27;-&#x27;.11-__&#x27;lzmd_ resulting from the principle referred to\nabove.\n\nWith a very important difference _(to be presently noticed)&#x27;. section [80\nalso carries out the principle referred to above.\n\nll. Juristic basis\n\n31.6. At this stage. an examination of the juristic basis of the principle of Juristic basis.\nelection is necessary. Historically speaking, there have been three suggestions\nas to the basis of the rule of election. namely, (a) implied condition_ (b) pre-\nsumed intention of the testutor. and (c) equity,\n\n(at) in the beginning, the view prevailed that the testutor gave his property\nto the recipient on the implied condition that the recipient would give his property\nto the third party? This explanation. however, is not accepted now.\n\n(b) Later, it theory was advanced that the doctrine of election W35 based\non the pmmmed imentiwz of the testator; but this theory was also rejected by the\nHouse of Lords&#x27;. &#x27;\n\ntel The explanation now accepted is that of equitable basis. lord Cairns\nexpressly observed&#x27; in I874 that the rule of election was not based eitheron the\ntestzitofs intention. or on his presumed intention, It is based on considerations\nof equity. This view was reiterated in 1920&#x27;&quot;.\n\n31.7. It is non generally accepted that in order to achieve a just result in presest undo&#x27;,\nthe circumstances of the case equity imposes this rule as kt matter of conscience. slat1dinginEngland.\n\nTo quote. Vieount Haldancis observations in Brown v. Gregson&quot; :\n\n&quot;The doctrine of election rests on a different foundation, it is a principle\nwhich the courts apply in the exercise or&#x27; an equitable jurisdiction enabling\nthem to secure a just distribution in substantial accordance with the general\nscheme of the instrument. It is not merely the language used to which the\n\ni(&#x27;]L1)(I.&#x27;l&#x27;:&quot;/(I\\ li&#x27;en:ir&#x27;.\\ t. This juristic basis~the basis of equitywdirectly leads to a consideration\nof certain aspects of the section in our Act. The precise question to be examined\nis. whether considerations of equity&#x27; necessitate total farfeitztre of the benefit\nwhich the will proposes to give to the unwilling legatee (as is the present law\nin India), or whether it is enough if the disappointed legatee is compensated (as\nis the law in England). In our view, it is enough if the unwilling legatee is permit-\ned to retain what the testator has left to him, on condition of compensating the\ndisappointed legateg for the loss of the bene\ufb01t which the tcstator intended for\nthe disappointed legatee. The present English law compels the refractory legatee\n(the Iegatee who does not comply with the will), merely to compensate the\ndisappointed legatee. The Indian law lays down forfeiture of the legacy proposed\nto be given to the legatce who does not comply with the will; and, in effect, the\nbalance goes to the residuary legatec of the testator. This is clear from illustration\n(it to section 182&#x27;.\n\nIll. Emphasis on intention misplaced\n\n31.9. When the matter is viewed in the above light. it becomes a question\nfor srious consideration whether the law in India, as incorporated in section\n180, should not be changed. The present law appears to beunduly harsh. It\nplaces too much emphasis on the le.s&#x27;tctt0r&#x27;.s&#x27; intention. The emphasis might have\nbeen derived from the Roman law&#x27;, but there is no reason why we should now\nadhere to that old doctrine. Gifts in contemplation of death constitute a subject analogous to the\nlaw of succession and are governed by the detailed provisions laid down in\nsection 191. &#x27;\n\nI&#x27;)!\n\nI9] (I) A man may dispose. by gift made in contemplation of death. of\nany movcable property which he could dispose of by will.\n\n32.2. Section reads as under :--\n\n..\n</pre>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_1\">(2) A gift is said to be made in contemplation of death where a man.\nwho is ill and expects to die shortly of his illness, delivers to another the\npossession of any moveable property to keep as a gift in case the donor\nshall die of that illness. &#x27;\n\n\n\nW7\n\n(3) Such a gift may be resumed by the gixci&quot;. and shall not take effect\nit he recovers from the illness during which it was made; nor if he survives\nthe person to whom it was made.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_2\">ILLUSTRATIONS\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_3\">(i) A. being ill. and in expectation of death. delivers to B. to be retained\nby him in case of A&#x27;s death--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_1\">&quot;a watch;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_2\">&quot;a bond granted by C to A;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_3\">&quot;a bank note :\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_4\">&quot;a promissory note of the Central Government endorsed in blank:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_5\">&quot;a bill of exchange endorsed in blank: certain mortgage--deeds.\n&quot;A dies of the illness during which he delivered these articles.\nB is entitled to--\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_6\">the watch;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_7\">the debt secured by Gs bond :\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_8\">the bank-note :\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_9\">the promissory note of the Central Government:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_10\">the bill of exchange:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_11\">the money secured by the mortgage-deeds.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_12\">(ii) A, being ill, and in expectation of death. delivers 10 B the key of a\ntrunk or the key of a warehouse in which goods of bulk belonging to A\ndeposited. with the intention of giving him the control over the contents of the\ntrunk or over the deposited goods and desires him to keep them in case of As\ndeath. A dies of the illness during which he delivered these articles. B is entitled\nto the trunk and its contents or to A&#x27;s goods of bulk in the warehouse.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_13\">(iii) A, being ill, and in expectation of death, puts aside certain articles\nin separate parcels and marks upon the parcels respectively the names of\nB and C. The parcels are not delivered during the life of A. A dies of\nthe illness during which he sets aside the parcels. B and C are not entitled\nto the contents of the parcel&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_4\">The illustrations may be thus explained.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_5\">Illustration (i) takes cases where there has been actual delivery in con-\ntemplation of death.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_6\">Illustration (ii) takes cases of constructive delivery.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_7\">Illustration (iii) is a case of no delivery.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_8\">32.3. Story in his Equity Jurisprudence points out that a gift of the nature Nature and 0,535,,\nprovided in section 191 is a sort of amphibious gift between a gift during the\nlife time and a legacy&#x27;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_9\">The origins of the concept of donatio m0rti.r causa are to be found in the\nRoman Law. The concept was found to be useful, and was adopted in English\nlaw. Following observations of Lord Cooper&quot; show the original rationale for\nthe rule.\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_14\">&quot;Where a man lies in extremity, or being surprised with sickness and not\nhaving an opportunity of making his will, but lest he should die before\nhe could make it, it gives by his own hands his goods to his friends about\nhim. This, if he dies, shall operate as a legacy, but if he recovers, then\ndoes the property revert to him&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_10\">Thus, delivery takes the place of a formal will. Incidentally, at present, it\nis not necessary that the donor must be in extremis at the time when the gift\nmortis causa is made&#x27;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_11\">&#x27;Story. Equity Jurisprudence (I919). page 256, Para 606. -\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_12\">&#x27;Lord Cooper in Hedges v. Hedges, (1908) Precedents In Chancery 269&#x27;. J. Gareth Miller\nVlachineryofsuccession (1977), page 275.\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_15\">&quot;Notein(1965)81L.Q.R.21.Seeparagraph32.4. ta) infra.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_16\">English law as to\ndonations mortis-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_17\">cause.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_18\">Delivery of\n\ndocument .\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_19\">Section 191-\nApplicability[toHin-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_20\">dus.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_21\">H_indu Law, as to\ngifts in contemp-\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_22\">lation of death.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_23\">Calcutta case.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_24\">l78\n\n32.4. Traditional Hindu law9. &quot;&#x27; deals with the situation as one of gift.\nand accordingly requires &quot;giving.-&quot; (with the requisite intention) and acceptance.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_18\">A rlwiutiu mt)rti.i&#x27; (&#x27;tl1l,\\&#x27;tl of movable property is recognised in Hindu&#x27; law.\nWest and Buhler&quot; state that &quot;a t&#x27;ather&#x27;s promises are looked on -as binding, unless\nthe performance of them would prevent the ful\ufb01lment of some still more sacred\nduty. But that the courts will not enforce. such obligation except subject to the\nconditions of the statute law where that is in force.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_19\">In at Bombay case&#x27;-&#x27;, A promised&#x27; lands to his daughter. but never executed\na deed of transfer. On his death-bed, he asked his son to give the lands to\nthe daughter and the son agreed. After his death, A&#x27;s will was found to contain\na bequest of all&#x27;his property to the son and C. Protection of the property of the &#x27;deceased forms the subject matter\nof sections 192 to 210. which create a machinery for such protection. Under\nsection 192. proceedings for the purpose can be initiated by a person claiming\na rightful succession. either after actual possession has been taken by another\nperson or when forcible means of seizing possession are apprehended, An\napplication for the purpose has to be made to the District Judge who, after\ninquiry under sections 193-194. has power, under section 194, to determine\nsummarily the right to possession and shall deliver possession accordingly. The\norder of the District Judge is subject to the right of rhc aggrieved party to\nbring a suit under section 208. Pending determination of the summary proceedings.\nthe District Judge has power to appoint a curator of the property. The rest\nof the chapter deals mainly with matter connected with the Curator.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_28\">Scope.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_29\">33.2. The section is deiivcd from an Act of 1841*. The principle underlying\nclarify 1116 pqsition the section is simple. A curator is an officer of the court, and suits instituted\n:l5m::&#x27;ma&quot;&quot;&#x27;\u00b0&quot;&#x27;-V \u00b0f by him are subject to control of the court. The question, however, is whether.\n&#x27; before a suit is instituted by the curator, he must obtain the leave of the court.\nThe section provides that a suit should be instituted or defended in the name of\nthe t&#x27;l(Iul()r. But, according to judicial interpretation, it is not necessary&#x27; that\nthe curator must be speci\ufb01cally authorised by the District Judge to institute or\ndefend the suit. The position. in this regard, should be codi\ufb01ed by adopting the\njudicial interpretation.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_65\">The proviso to section 200 requires that if a curator is appointed to collect\ndebts or rents of property. the order appointing him as curator must confer express\nauthority to give discharge for the moneys received. The proviso does not relate\nto the institution or defence of suits. but to the collection of debts and rents.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_66\">Recommendation 33.13. Another inat_ter_on _which the law could be suitably codi\ufb01ed relates\n\nto clarify me push to the need for _a succession certi\ufb01cate when the curator takes proceedings. it has\n\ntion as to succes- been held3 that it 15 not necessary for the curator to obtain a succession certi\ufb01cate\n\n9i0n certi\ufb01cate before instituting a suit to recover the debt. This pOSitiOn also should be made\nclear by an amendment of section 200. An Explanation could be added for the\npurpose.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_67\">_ 33.14. in the light of what we have stated above we recommend that\n,S,,u\u00a7,g.ff:i-\u00b05d\u00a2:,r;&#x27;2,&#x27;,&#x27;,&quot;:a_i(&#x27;)((1)_ the following Explanations should be added below section,200 :-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_48\">&quot;Explanation 1&#x27;: It is I10] necessary that the curator must be speci\ufb01cally\nauthorised by the I)i.vtrirt Judge to institute or defend the suit.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_49\">lixplanation 2 ; It is not nece.ssz.r_v for the curator to obtain a succession\ncerti\ufb01cate before imtittltirig a wit to rec0t&#x27;er the debt.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_68\">gcmon 20._ 33.15. SECTIONS 211 T0 216\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_92\">1. Introductory\n\n34.1. Representatix-we title to the property of the deceased on succession\nis an important topic of the law of succession, dealt with in sections 211 to\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_93\">216.\n\nThe Cliapter begins with a positive provision in section Zll. which enacts\nthat the executor or administrator of a deceased person is his legal representative\nfor all purposes. and all property of the deceased vests in him as such. In case\nof an admiiiistrator_ the very de\ufb01nition of that expression&#x27; requires that he must\nbe appointed by a competent authority. As regards an executor, section 213i <a href=\"/doc/1066951/\" id=\"a_683\">1</a> )\nprovides, inter utlia, that (in cases where that section applies) no right as\n3 executor can be established in a court without obtaining probate or letters of\n\nadministration with the will annexed. Thus, in soine form or other. the court\nenters into the picture in most cases.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_94\">3-1.2. The position in India under the Act otlers a contrast to the continental\nsystem. In most countries of Europe. where in modern times. the in\ufb02uence of\nRoman law has been predominant. property passes on immediately to the bene-\n\ufb01ciaries. who by their acceptance of the bene\ufb01t become directly liable for the\npayment .of the deceaseds debts, In England, however, this is not the case&#x27;.\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_68\">&quot;With us. the property -must first vest in personal representatives whose duty\nit is to administer the estate by paying debts and distributing the residue amongst\nthe persons bene\ufb01cially entitled. Moreover, their authority to administer must\nbe rati\ufb01ed or created by what is in fact and in law an order of court. and this\norder of court (either probate or letters of administration) becomes an essential\nlink in the title of the beneficiaries.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_95\">Origin of cxecum, 34.3. The conception of_ the personal i&#x27;cpi&#x27;ese11tative is founded up.on the\nand administrator, position of the executor appointed by the testator&#x27;s will to execute its terms, an\ninstitution prevalent throughout Europe in the Middle Ages and perhaps traceable\nto the ancient German Salman to whom property was transferred in order that\nhe might carry out the wishes of the transferer. Where no executor was appointed.\nthe medieval Church claimed the right to administer_ though in practice the\nBishop appointed some member of the deceased&#x27;s family t by granting to him\nletters of administration) to act as administrator in his place. This practice was\n(in England) made conipulsory by statute as early as 1357, and thereafter the\nposition and functions of the administrator have been practically assimilated\n\nto those of the executor.&#x27;\nScheme of mmm 34.4, The scheme of these sections. is that where those sections apply,\n&#x27; &#x27; it becomes necessary to obtain the appropriate document conferring or recognising\n\nScope of the Chap-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_96\">ter.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_97\">Eitecutors and ad\u00ab\nministrators.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_98\">212-214. _ _ _ _\nthe t&#x27;eprescnt1ltl\\&#x27;C llliC_ before a right can be established to the property of\nthe deceased.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_99\">ll. Survivorship and the question of shares\nSection 211(1) 34.5, We may now take up the sections for detailed consideration, Section\n\n211(1), provides that the executor or administrator of a deceased is his legal\n\n1Section 20:)&#x27;: &#x27;&#x27;Administrator&#x27;&#x27;.\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_69\">&quot;Stephen, Commentaries on the Law of England (I950) Vol, I. page 520.\nVstephcn, (&#x27;ommentaries on the Law of England (1950), Vol. ], page 520.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_70\">\u00abw\n\n16:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_71\">representative for all purposes. and all the property of the deceased person\nvests in him as such. But, as regards Hindus, Mohammadans. Buddhists, Sikhs.\nJains, Parsees and other persons, sub--section (2) provides that nothing in the\nsection shall vest in the executor or administrator my property of the deceased\nperson which would otherwise have passed by survivorship to some other\nperson.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_72\">34.6. This naturally raises the question whether letters of administration Survivorship in res-\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_100\">can be obtained in respect of property passing by .\\&#x27;m&#x27;viv0rs/&#x27;1i,r2 to copareeners D\u00a2\u00b0l\u00b0f5h3Y\u00b05-\n\nin a Hindu undivided family. Much of the case law on the subject relates to\n\nshares in companies&#x27;. ln general. property passing by survivorship is distinguished\n\nfrom property passing by succession. The distinction. in fact. is elementary and\n\nsound. But this theoretical distinction creates certain practical dilticulties, because\n\nof the fact that under the statutory provisions applicable&quot; to shares in companies.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_101\">shares are registered only in the name of a particular person. and only the\n\nperson registered is recognised as the &quot;legal owner&quot; of those shares so far\n\nas the company is concerned. Now. these statutory provisions create sortie problems in regard to Shares standing in\nshares belonging to the coparceners of a Hindu undivided family and standing the name 07 the\nin the name of one cloparcener--the manager. When the registered &quot;legal&quot; manage&quot;\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_73\">owner-----i.e. the manager--dies. how is the title of the surviving coparceners\nto be established on his death 2&#x27;\n\n34.8. Where. as a matter of general law, the right to shares passes by Applicability of sec\nsurvivorship, the provisions of sections 211 to 213 would not, if construed tions 2&quot;-313 10\nliterally, be applicable to the situation. But if such a rigid view of the law is 5h&quot;&quot;9&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_103\">taken, then there remains no machinery for establishing, to the satisfaction\nof the company, the title of the persons succeeding by survivorship. By and\nlarge, courts have, taking a realistic view. construed sections 211 to 213\nwidely for the purpose. There is, no doubt. some conflict of views on the\nquestion as to the basis on which court fees are to be calculated&quot; in respect of\npetitions for latters of administration \ufb01led by the surviving coparceners. That\nc0n\ufb01ict_ however as it pertains to the <a href=\"/doc/138097064/\" id=\"a_684\">Court Fees Act</a>. is not material for our\npurpose.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_104\">34.9 Our primary concern is with the question whether letters of adminis- Leuersor .,dm,&#x27;m5.\ntration can be granted in respect of shares in joint stock companies where the tration regarding\nshares stand in the name of the karta of a Mitakshara Joint Hindu family. On Shares-\nthat question. most High Courts take the view* that in the case of such shares\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1353758/\" id=\"a_685\">Companies Act</a>. (and usually, the Articles of Association of the Company).\nmake it abundantly clear that vis-a-vis the company. the local title in the\nshares is in the registered holder of the shares_ viz. the karta of the joint family\nin whose name the shares stand. He is the only person recognised by the company\nas having the shares. The position. therefore. is that on the death of the karta\nwho was the registered holder of the shares. the title to the shares vis--a--vis\nthe company, does not, on death of the karta. without the grant of letters of\nadministration, pass by .s&#x27;ur\\&#x27;/&#x27;varsliip to the surviving members ol the coparcenary\nof which the deceased was the karta. So far as the company 13 mm&#x27;erned, the\nexcep\ufb01gng in cugcg of Hindu dying intestate. provided in <a href=\"/doc/37219/\" id=\"a_686\">Section 2</a>| 1(2) and\n212(2) of the <a href=\"/doc/1450343/\" id=\"a_687\">Indian Succession Act</a>. l925. do not. therefore. apply in such\ncases. At the same time, neither the principles of Hindu law nor the provisions\ncontained in the <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_688\">Succession Act</a>. stand in the uay of the grant of the Letters of\nAdministration limited to the shares held by the karta of a joint l\\4ital&lt;shara\nHindu family. in the event of his dying intestate.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_105\">&#x27;Paras 34.8 and 34. 9. infra.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_106\">&#x27;Thc Companies Act. 1956.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_107\">&#x27;Section l9D. Establishment of right to property of deceased\n\n34.16. Section 212(1) provides that no right to any part of the property section 212\nof :1 person who dies intestate can be established in any court of justice unless\nletters of administration have first been granted by a court of competent\njurisdiction. Sub-section (2) provides that this section shall not apply in the\ncase ofintestacy of a Hindu. Mohammadan, Buddhist, Sikh, Jain, Indian Christian\n\nor Parsee.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_89\">The section needs no change.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_90\">34.17. Section 213 provides that before title (as an executor) can besection 2l3--\nestablished, probate of the will must be obtained. There seems to be some E.5&#x27;ab&#x27;.i5hm\u00b0&quot;&#x27; ,\u00b0f\nuncertainty as to whether, in cases where probate is not obtained, (though-&#x27;me&#x27; \u00b0x\u00b0\u00b0&#x27;&quot;\u00b0r&#x27;\nrequired by the section), the provisions of the section merely bar the passing\nof a decree on the basis of a right claimed under the will, or whether they bar\nthe very institution of the suit to enforce that right.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_91\">The Madras and Patna High Courts take the former view&#x27;, namely, only\nthe passing of the decree is prohibited. The Andhra and Calcutta.High Courts&#x27;\ntake the latter view, namely, the very institution of the suit is barred.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_92\">It should be noted that in the case of an executor, the title depends on the\nwill, and not on the probate. Probate is needed only to prove title&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_120\">34.18. Whatever be the correct construction of the present wording of Recommendation\nsection 213, it seems to us that on practical considerations it is desirable to to amend Section\nadopt the former view&#x27;, which would bar only the passing of a decree. It re\ufb02ects 2&quot;-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_121\">a less technical approach than the cont-rary view. We therefore recommend that\nsection 213 should be amended by providing that where probate has not been\nobtained, what is barred is only the passing of a decree&#x27;. and not the institution\n\nof a suit.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_122\">34.18A, It has been represented to us, in one of the comments received section 213- ex-\non the Working Paper&quot;, that while Hindus and Muslims can get relief from the cluster; of Indian\ncourt on the basis of a will, Christians have to get a probate or letters of adminis\u00e9 Chglsllans &#x27;\u00b0\u00b0\u00b0mm&#x27;\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_123\">-tration for getting relief on the basis of a will. It has been suggested in that \u00b0&quot; a&quot;\u00b0&quot;s&#x27;\ncommunication that Christians should be excluded from section 213. This is a\nsuggestion forwarded to us along with the letter of the Catholic Bishop&#x27;s Con-\nference of India. We accept the suggestion, though the point was not contained\n1 in our Working Papers. We recommend that (a) section 213 should be amended\nas above; and (b) consequential changes be made, wherever necessary, in other\nsections of the Act.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_124\">IV. Debts---need for succession certi\ufb01cate\n\n34.19. This takes us to section 214, with reference [0 which a number <a href=\"/doc/37219/\" id=\"a_699\">section 2</a>]4_ph1-age\nof points require consideration. The phrase did not\noccur in the earlier Act of 1865, but was added by the Joint Committee that\nconsidered the Bill which led to the present Act.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_126\">The signi\ufb01cance of these words will be soon realised when we consider\nthe cases where a claim is made against the &#x27;debtor of a deceased person&#x27; on the\n\n&#x27;(a) In re Ramchand A.I.R. 1956 Mad. 274, 276.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_127\">\\b) Bhude v. It appears to be desirable to provide\nthat a succession certi\ufb01cate can be issued in respect of an amount due under\nan insurance policy.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_105\">34.21. in particular, with reference to <a href=\"/doc/68009942/\" id=\"a_704\">section 6(1)</a> oi&#x27; the <a href=\"/doc/51897370/\" id=\"a_705\">Married\nWomen&#x27;s Property Act, 1874</a>, it may be of interest to note that a succession\ncerti\ufb01cate is not required when a trust is created under that section&quot;. The\nsection constitutes a speci\ufb01c statutory provision which creates a trust. On such\na trust coming into being, the policy of insurance ceases to form part of the\nestate of the deceased. The concluding words of section (&gt;( I) of the above\nmentioned Act, in fact, so provide. Although the point does not necessitate\nany amendment of <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_706\">section 2l4</a> of the Succession Act, it is worth noting in the\ncontext of the subject under discussion.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_106\">34.22. The most important problem created by section 214(1) is a practical\n\none. The section has been resorted to by banks and other persons for refusing\n\npayment of a debt or a claim owed to a deceased person where a succession\ncertificate is not produced. It was never intended that the section should, in this\nmanner, come in the way of the heirs of the deceased person, The object of\nthe section is merely to niake it clear that no debt due to a deceased person\ncan be recovered through court except by a holder of a document of the cate-\ngory&#x27;. However, practical experience shows that a provision which was intended\nto have application mainly in relation to litigation has had the unintended effect\nof causing hardship to the heirs of deceased persons.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_107\">34.23. Presumably, it was not the intention to lay down 21 rule of law\nthat 3 bank or other debtor can ever make a payment to the person who claims\nto be the heir unless he produces the succession certi\ufb01cate or other document\nspeci\ufb01ed in the section. Common experience, however, shows that banks and\nother institutions do take such a stand, causing immense hardship to the heirs.\npzirticularly to poor widows and children of deceased persons\n\n34.24. Where the debt or claim is of such a nature that there is a statutory\nprovision for nomination and that provision has been actually utilised by the\ndeceased persons. then, in practice, the banks and other institutions make pay-\nmom to the nominee. Btit in the absence of such a statutory nomination, great\n\nhardship is caused to the heirs.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_108\">34.25. The position is anomalous in another respect also, inasmuch as the\nlaw imposes no such restriction in the case of title to immovable property.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_109\">&#x27;Section 38. InsuranccAct. I938.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_110\">&quot;l&quot;.V.Subramania, &#x27;ls lnsuranccadebt&quot;. A.l.R. 1927 Journal 14.\n&quot;PaIne.s&#x27;lm&#x27;uri&#x27; Bai V. Ni/ml Cham/. A.l.R. 1938 Sind. 20.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_111\">&#x27;See also in Re Aslm Lam Desai, A.l.R. 1940 Cal. 21 7.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_112\">&#x27;KI&#x27;ssen Lal V. Ti/ak Chandra, A.l.R. 1940 Cal. 24.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_7\">189</span>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_113\">A person claiming to be the heir of a deceased owner of &#x27;nntnovable property\ncan continue as owner of the immovable property of the deceased, without pro-\nducing a succession certi\ufb01cate. but he cannot withdraw moneys but by the\ndeceased in a bank account------and this is so even where there is no doubt that\nhe is the heir. Same is the position regarding tenant. Of course, a tenant has\nalso to bear the liability to pay rent. but the fact remains that he gets a valuable\nadvantage without being required to produce a document establishing his right\nto succeed. It is understandable that a bank may like to have some evidence\nof death and of the claimant&#x27;s right before it makes payment, but there could be\nother evidence-----i.e. evidence other than a succession certi\ufb01cate.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_114\">it seems, therefore, desirable to recommend some reform that would solve\nthe above problem. While it cannot be provided that the claim must in every\ncase be paid without a succession certi\ufb01cate, it would be reasonable at least to\nprovide that a payment without such certi\ufb01cate is not illegal.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_115\">34.25A. We note that by a recent amendment&#x27; made in the Banking Rt5gll~ Rccemgmcndmemg\nlation Act, 1949, it is now possible to make a nomination in respect of deposits made in banking\nin banks. Where such nominations are made, the hardship otherwise experienced law.&#x27; 35 *0 &quot;\u00b0mi&#x27;\nby the relatives of a deceased depositor would be alleviated to some extent, &quot;anon&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_131\">since the bank would be protected and would get a full discharge on making\npayment to the nominee. But, in cases where no such nomination has been made,\nthe diflicultics felt by virtue of\u00abthe practice of banks to which reference has been\nalready made&quot; will survive. An amendment of the <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_707\">Succession Act</a> would, there-\nfore, possess considerable utility. even after the amendment of the banking\nlaws.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_132\">Incidentally, it may be mentioned that a nomination does not affect the Em,&quot; of nomim.\nrights of the other heirs, and, as between the nominee and the rival heirs, the tion.\nnominee does not get any higher or preferential status. The new sections inserted\nin banking laws provide so expressly.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_133\">34.25B. We may also note that in a Kerala case reported recently&quot;, the Section 214 and\n\nwidow of a person who had been killed in an accident caused by the driver of small claims,&#x27;\na truck, claimed compensation against the Kerala State Road Transport Corpo-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_134\">ration, which owned the truck. Presumably the claim was under the <a href=\"/doc/594667/\" id=\"a_708\">Fatal\n\nAccidents Act</a> read with the <a href=\"/doc/785258/\" id=\"a_709\">Motor Vehicles Act</a>. Resisting the claim, the State\n\nRoad Transport Corporation insisted that the widow should produce a succession\n\ncerti\ufb01cate before payment could be made to her. The widow thereupon moved\n\nthe High Court of Kerala by a letter, which was treated as a petition. The High\n\nCourt pointed out that no succession certi\ufb01cate was required in this case, as\n\nthe widow was claiming in her own right, and not as an heir, As regards the\n\ncases where the widow (or other relative.) claims as an heir, the High Court\n\nmade the following observations, which are relevant to the provisions of the\n\n<a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_710\">Succession Act</a> :-----\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_116\">&quot;It is for the legislature to deal with the matter by enabling payments to be\nmade, at least in respect of small sums of money wit/rout a succession\ncerti\ufb01cate being required to give immunity to the debtor. We are not going\ninto that question in this case, for, even, otherwise, we think we will be\nable to give relief to the petitioner herein.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_135\">34.26. For these reasons, in our opinion, the appropriate course would Recommenda\ufb01on\nbe to add to section 214, an Explanation lll suitable terms so as to ensure :9 add an Explana-\nthat the section will not--as is the practice at present--be utilised as a shelter &quot;On to Section 214\n\nin everv case for deceased &#x27;$5 b*\u00b0 PaY&quot;&#x27;\u00b0&quot;&#x27; \u00b0f&#x27;\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_136\">- &#x27; e t.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_137\">We recommend that the section should be amended accordingly&#x27;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_138\">&#x27;The Banking Laws Amendment Act. i983.\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_117\">&quot;Paragraph 34. 22. sppra.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_118\">(J 3Kocbupenm( I.nksh/m&#x27; V. (&#x27;lmirmam K.S.R. T. cor/mrarinn, AJR. 1984 Ker. 97. 99, para 4\nune).\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_139\">&#x27;See para 34 . 45, I&#x27;n\ufb01&#x27;a, for a suggested re-draft ofthe section.\n</p>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_8\">190</span>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_140\">VI. Scope of the expression &quot;Debt&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_141\">Meaning of &quot;debt&quot; 34.2&#x27;/&quot;._There seems to be a con\ufb02ict of decisions on the exact scope of\nl3_0$ba.V and Patna_tlie expression &quot;debt&quot; in sections 214, 370, 372(l)(t) and 381. The conflict\n&quot;&#x27; * 1S apparent from a Bombay Judgment&#x27;, which has discussed the matter at length.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_142\">dissenting from an Allahabad case*, and agreeing with a Patna case&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_143\">1n the Patna&#x27; case. the appellant applied. under section 370, for a succession\ncertificate in respect of certain ornaments pledged with the Punjab National\nBank at Darbhanga. The High Court dismissed the application, holding that\nthe expression &quot;debts and securities&quot; (in sections 370 and 381 )5 did not cover\npledged ornaments,\n\nAllahabad view. 34.28. In the Allahabad case&#x27;, one lady had died in the Kumbha Mela\ntragedy and the district authorities had taken possession of certain cash and\nornaments from the body of the deceased. When the petitioners, as her heirs,\nasked for the return of valuables, the authorities insisted on the production\nof a succession certi\ufb01cate. Consequently, the petitioners approached the court\nfor the purpose. The -question arose whether &quot;do.-ht&quot; would cover pledged\nmovables. The High Court held that the term &quot;debt&quot; meant a liability owing\nfrom one person to another whether in cash or kind, secured or unsecured,\nwhether ascertained or ascertainable, arising out ot&#x27; any obligation, express or\nimplied. The refund of ornaments recovered from the dead body became an\nobligation of the authorities who were bound to hand over the same to the\nperson entitled. Accordingly, a succession certi\ufb01cate was granted.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_144\">Bombay case. 34.29. The Bombay case&#x27; discusses the problem of interpretation of the\nterm &#x27;debt&#x27; at great length. In that case, one lady had died in 1963, leaving\nbehind her husband and three sons. Her husband died in 1966, leaving behind\nhis three sons. The lady had taken a demand loan of Rs. 3.000 from the State\nBank of India on the security of certain ornaments. The sons paid off the\nloan amount with interest to the Bank, and claimed return of the goldpledged.\nThe Bank informed them that it could not reurn the jewellery in the absence\nof a succession certi\ufb01cate. The sons made an application for a succession certi-\n\ufb01cate. The High Court, construing the term &#x27;debt&#x27; narrowly, held that a.certi-\n\ufb01cate could not be granted in respect of the ornaments pledged with the Bank.\nThereupon, the petitioner sought leave of the court to withdraw the application.\nwhich was allowed.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_145\">Recommendation 34,30, In this position. it is desirable to settle the law on the subject.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_146\">&#x27;\u00b0 5\u00b0&quot;&#x27;\u00b0 the &#x27;&#x27;&#x27;&#x27;w&#x27; Although, in general, &quot;debt&quot; could be taken as con\ufb01ned to a monetary obligation\nfor a liquidated amount, practical considerations require that the expression\n&quot;debt&quot; should, for the purposes of sections 214, 370(1) and 381, be de\ufb01ned\nas including any actionable claim.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_147\">We recommend that the law should be amended on the above lines.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_148\">Of course, the ellect of the proposed amendment would he to extend the\nrestrictive as well as the bene\ufb01cial provisions of the Act to actionable claims.\nBut that would he the only logical course.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_149\">Gist of the re1e- 34.3]. It may be mentioned for ready reference that section 370(1) pro-\nva_ntsectf0nsSumm&#x27;v&#x27;ides that a succession certi\ufb01cate shall not be granted with respect to any\n&quot;&#x27;9&#x27;-&#x27;d&#x27; &quot;debt&quot; or security to which a right is required by section 212 or section 213\n\nto be established by letters of administration or probate. Section 372(l)(f)\nprovides, inter alia, that an application for succession certi\ufb01cate shall set forth\nthe &quot;debts&quot; and securities in respect to which the certi\ufb01cate is applied for. Section\n381 provides that the succession certi\ufb01cate shall, with respect to the &quot;debts&quot; and\n\n&#x27;Ranchoddas v. Gavind Das, (1976) 78 Born. LR. 2l9.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_9\">191</span>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_120\">securities speci\ufb01ed therein, be conclusive as against the persons owing such debts\nor ltableon such securities, and shall afford full indemnity to all persons who\nin good faith make payment to the person to whom the certificate was granted.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_121\">\\/ll. Slate for obtaining succession certi\ufb01cate.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_122\">34.32. The stage at which probate or succession certilicate should be ob-- Stage for obtaining\n\nrained has also become the&#x27; subject of controversy. in one Allahabad case&#x27;.3Fa&quot;&#x27;4\n\nthe question for consideration was whether at probate or succession certi\ufb01cate\n\nwas required to be obtained by the plaintiffs, before a decree could be granted\n\nin their favour. An Explanation on the add 31! Explaglatioll\nsubject could be usefully added to the section*&#x27;_ for the purpose. :&#x27;:&#x27;.:\u00b0 &#x27;&quot;&quot;&quot;&#x27;&quot;&#x27;\u00b0&quot; \u00b0f\n\nVIII. J oint decrees.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_154\">34.34. Another question concerning the provisions of section 214 arises Joint scenes,\nin connection with joint decrees. Where the decree was passed jointly in favour\nof more than one decree-holder, and each one of them in his own right could\nexecute the decree (provided the execution is for the bene\ufb01t of all)&#x27;, the bar in\nsection 214 does not apply&#x27;. Section 214, it has been held, can apply only when\nthere is one decree--ho1der. Where there is only one decree-holder, taken on the\ndeath of the decree-holder, the legal representatives have no right to take out\nexecution proceedings unless they have stepped into the shoes of the decree-\nholder by obtaining a succession certi\ufb01cate. In a Rajasthan case&#x27;, there was\nonly one decree-holder. and in order to avoid future conflicts between rival\nclaimants, the bar of section 214 was held to apply. An Assam case&#x27;, is to the\nsame effect.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_155\">IX. Continuation of proceedings.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_156\">34.35. With reference to section 2l4(l)(b), a controversy has arisen S\u20acCti0n_ 214 (1) (b)-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_157\">whether it bars the continuation of an execution proceeding without obtaining iIi&quot;\u00b0\u00b0&quot;&quot;\u00b0&quot; p&#x27;\u00b0\u00b0\u00b0\u00b0d&#x27;\na succession certi\ufb01cate. According to one view&#x27;-&#x27;\u00b0, the main purpose of section g&quot;\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_124\">214 is to protect the debtor from vexatious proceedings and from being harassed\nat different times by different persons claiming to be successors; the continuation\nof an e.recun&#x27;on proceeding by the legal representatives is not hit by the section.\nand a succession certi\ufb01cate need not be produced in such :1 case. according to\nthis View.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_125\">&#x27;Blzwtjuji \\&#x27;. Iugerlzwar Dayal, A.[.R. 1978 All. 268.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_126\">&quot;See case law discussed. para 33 . 32. supra,\n\n&quot;Para 34 . 45, I&#x27;n_/i&#x27;:I.\n\n&#x27;NandLaiv. But, where no subszamive application is initiated by the legal\nrepresentatives, mere continuation of the execution proceedings already initiated\nby the plaintiff decree-holder is not barred&#x27;-&quot;. It has also been pointed out that\nunder 0.22. r. 2. C.l&#x27;.C. fresh application is not required.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_158\">According to a contrary view&#x27;--&quot;&#x27; an application by a legal representative to\nbring himself on record in a pending case should be treated as an application for\nfresh execution, and thtrefore attracts the provisions of section 2144.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_159\">According to a recent Kerala judgmenti. section 2l4 applies to the continu-\nation of execution proceedings.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_160\">Recommendation 34.36. Whatever be the true construction of the present wording, practical\n:9 add an Explana_- considerations require that the former view should be adopted, i.c. the continuation\n&quot;\u00b0&quot;. &quot;Sf&quot; \u00b0\u00b0!&quot;&quot; of execution proceedings by the legal representative should not be hit by the\ng::&#x27;:\u00e9;:\ufb01\u00a3]g\u00b0sx\u00b0\u00b0&quot;&quot;\u00b0&quot; section. A suitable Explanation should be added to deal with the matter&#x27;. No\n&#x27; doubt, the object of section 214(1) is to protect the interest of the debtor and\nto see that only the person entitled to succeed to the assets realises the debt\nowing to the deceased. Nevertheless, there is a limit to which one can give\neffect to this logic. Considerations of expeditious disposal. must, at_some stage,\nprevail. If a \\yrong person conti}r!1ues the execution. the rightful heir can claim\n\nthe fruits of t e recovery from im.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_161\">X. Mortgage decrees.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_162\">Secured debts posi- 34.37. Certain questions have arisen as to whether a decree (preliminary\n\n&quot;W &#x27;eluding m\u00b0l&quot;or \ufb01nal) for the foreclosure or sale of property can be passed wthout a succession\n\n&quot;&quot;3\u00b0d\u00b0\u00b0&#x27;\u00b0\u00b05&#x27; certi\ufb01cate where the mortgagee has died. Should the legal representative be\nasked to produce a succession certi\ufb01cate before obtaining such a decree? Section\n214 does not expressly include within its scope a decree for the enforcement of\nthe mortgagee&#x27;s rights against the mortgaged property.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_163\">Foreclosure. 34.38. On a detailed examination of the case law, the position appears to\nbe this, A decree for the foreclosure of a mortgage is not one for the payment\nof a &quot;debt&quot; so as to fall within the provision of section 214. The direction to\npay within the period \ufb01xed in the decree, is given not to fix a personal liability\nfor the debt, but to enable the defendants to save their right of redemption and\nto prevent its extinction by foreclosure&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_164\">Money due under 34.39.  suit to recover the money due under a usufruct_uar__v mortgage is\nusufnlctuary m0r- also not a suit for &quot;debt&quot; or money due under a contract, but is, in reality, one\n&#x27;3&#x27;5&#x27;- for assessment in money of the value of the plaintiffs mortgage interest&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_165\">Redemption. 34.40. A suit for redemption (brought by the heir of the mortgagor&#x27;) is also\nnot a suit against a debtor to obtain a decree for payment of his &quot;debt&quot;. The\nplaintiff is really the debtor, and not the creditor of the deceased mortgagor,\nObviously, therefore. a person who, as heir of the mortgagor, is bringing a suit\nfor redemption, is not suing to recover any debt, and a succession certi\ufb01cate\nis not required&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_166\">1Benon&#x27;ev. Pnrnemlu, A.l.R. l973 Cal. 352, 353,(A.K. Sarirar, 1.).\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_167\">3.5&#x27;:-r&#x27;na!.7r KImndr&gt;7wa.&#x27;. V. According to the majority of the High Courts, :1 suit to recover money Suits for Sale of mo-\ndue on a simple mortgage, by sale of the mortgaged property, is also not a suit &#x27;waged 9&#x27;\u00b0&quot;\u00b0&quot;&#x27;Y-\nfor the recovery of a &#x27;debt&#x27;. On this view.\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_136\">&quot;debt&quot; would not cover a decree for the enforcement of the mortgagee&#x27;s rights\nagainst mortgaged property.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_137\">34.42. The Allahabad High Court, in its earlier cases, expressed the opinion\nthat money lent on the security of a mortgage is a &quot;debt&quot; due from the mortgagor\nto the mortgagee, and the decree in a suit on such mortgage is a decree for the\npayment of &quot;debt&quot;?---i.e. payment by sale of the mortgaged property. No\nsuccession certi\ufb01cate was, therefore, necessary. T be earlier Allahabad rulings\nare not cited in the judgment.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_139\">34.43. It seems to us that but for the early rulings ol the Allahabad High True pog.&#x27;u&#x27;on_\nCourt, the position is well established that a decree for sale is not a decree for\n&quot;debt&quot;. The later Allahabad judgment also discloses-\u00abif indirectly\u00bb-21 changed\ntrend. In this position. a clari\ufb01cation of the law is not required.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_140\">34.44.
