Recommendation as to section 214.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_186\">34.45. We have disposed of the various questions relating to section 214.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_187\">in For carrying out the points made so far in earlier paragraphs with reference to\n\n2l5.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_188\">216.\n\nsection 214, we recommend the insertion of the following Explanations to the\nsection \n\n&quot;Explanation li.--~Nothing in this section shall be construed as precluding\na debtor of a deceased person from making payment of a debt to a person\n(in this Explanation referred to as the &#x27;payee&quot;) claiming, on succession,\nto be entitled to the effects of the deceased person or to any part thereof,\nwhere the debtor is satis\ufb01ed that the payee is so entitled and has taken\nfrom the payee a bond indemnifying the debtor; and where a debtor makes\nsuch payment in good faith and after due care and attention, he shall not be\nbound to make payment of the debt again to the person entitled to the effects\nof the deceased person or to any part thereof, as the case may be, but\nnothing in this Explanation shall affect any remedy which the person so\ncntitlegi may have against the payee who has received payment from the\ndebtor.&quot;\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_147\">&quot;Explanation 2.--In this section, in sections 212 and 370, in sub-sectoin (1)\nof section 372 and in section 379, &#x27;debt&#x27; includes any actionable claim, and\n&quot;debtor&quot; and &quot;payment&quot; shall be construed accordingly&#x27;-&#x27;.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_148\">&quot;Explanation 3.----Nothing in this section shall be construed as precluding\nthe institution of a suit for the recovery of any debt, but no decree shall\nbe passed in a suit for such recovery unless the provisions of this section\nare complied with&#x27;.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_149\">&quot;Explanation 4.----Nothing in this section shall be construed as barring the&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_189\">continuance by a legal representative of a proceeding for execution already\ninitiated&#x27;.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_190\">X11. Miscellaneous.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_191\">34.46. This takes us to section 215, which reads as under :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_150\">&quot;2l5(l )---A grant of prob_ate or letters of administration in respect of an\nestate shall be deemed to supersede any certi\ufb01cate previously granted under\nPart X or under the Succession Certi\ufb01cate Act, 1889, or Bombay Regulation\n\nNo. VIII of 1827, in respect of any debts or securities included in the\n\nestate.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_151\">(2) When at the time of the grant of the Probate or letters any suit or\nother proceedings instituted by the holder of any such certi\ufb01cate regarding\nany such debt or security is pending, the person to whom the grant is\nmade shall, on applying to the Court in which the suit or proceeding is\npending, be entitled to take the place of the holder of the certi\ufb01cate in the\nsuit or proceeding :\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_152\">&quot;Provided that, when any certi\ufb01cate is superseded under this section, all\npayments made to the holder of such certi\ufb01cate in ignorance of such super-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_153\">session shall he held good against claims under the probate or letters of _\n\nadministration.&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_192\">The section seems to need no change.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_193\">34.47. According to section 216, after any grant of probate or letters of\n\nadministration, no person other than the person to whom the same may have\nbeen granted shall have power to sue or prosecute any suit, or otherwise act as\n\n&#x27;lf so considered necessary, the first could be limited to claims for small amounts.\n&#x27;See para 34. 36, supra.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_194\">3The de\ufb01nition of &quot;debt&quot; should, besides being incorporated in section 214, also be made\n\napplicable to sections 212, 370. 372(1) and 379,\n\n&#x27;See para 34. 30, supra.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_195\">&#x27;See para 34. 33. supra.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_196\">&#x27;See para 34.36, supra.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_197\">{JR\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_14\">19</span>\n\nrepresentative of the deceased, throughout the State in which the same may have\nbeen granted. until such probate or letters of administration has or have been\nrecalled or revoked.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_198\">This section also needs no change, having created no serzous problems.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_199\">CHAPTER 35\n\nPROBATE, LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION AND ADMlNIS&#x27;I&#x27;RATION\nOF ASSETS OF THE DECEASED\n\nSECTION 217--236\n\n35.1. Probate, letters ot administration and 21(.lII1lI&#x27;lis&#x27;lL&#x27;uli0n of assets and Scope.\nliabilities of the deceased, form the subject matter of sections 217 to 236. The\nsections are mostly of a procedural character.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_200\">35.2. Section 217, the first section in this group, provides that save as section 217_\notherwise provided by this Act or by any other law for the time being in force.\nall grants of probate and letters of administration with the will annexed shall\nbe made and the administration of assets of the deceased in cases of intestate\nsuccession shall be carried out, in accordance with the provisions of this Part.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_201\">The section needs no change, being a purely introductory provision.\n35.3. The next section 218 reads as under :-- sccmm 213,\n\n&quot;&#x27;2l8(l) If the deceased has died intestate and was :1 Hindu, Muhammadan,\nBuddhist, Sikh or Jain or an exempted person, administration of his estate\nmay be granted to any person who, according to the rules for the distribution\nof the estate applicable in the case of such deceased. would beentitled to\nthe whole or any part of such deceased&#x27;s estate.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_202\">(2) When several such persons apply for such administration. it shall be\nin the discretion of the court to grant it to any one or more of them.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_203\">(3) When no such person applies, it may be granted to a creditor or\nthe deceased.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_204\">35.4. In the context of this section, we propose to consider one question Joint fan-my pm.\nthat has arisen. Where joint family property passes by survivorship to the sole petty passing by\nsurviving co--parcener, the question arises whether the sole surviving co-parcener S&quot;fV|V\u00b0fShIp-\nis entitled to apply for letters of administration under section 218, ls he a person\n&#x27;who would be entitled to the whole or part of the estate of the deceased&#x27; within\nthe meaning of the section? There seems to be a conflict of decisions on the\n\nsubject.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_205\">35.5. A Madras case&#x27; answers the above, question in the atiirmative, though Conflict of news.\nthe observations in the case may be regarded as obiter. But. according to the\nPunjab view, if the deceased person and the present P\u20actitioner were members of\na joint family, then, immediately on the death of the deceased, his interest in\nthe estate at once ceases and the whole interest in the estate belongs to the\npetitioner by survivorship, and there is no &quot;estate of the deceased&quot; to be adminis-\ntered: there is no &quot;succession&quot; to the deceased&#x27;s estate, because he has left\nnothing to succeed to. Qonsequently. an application for letters of administration\n\nis not competent in such cases?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_206\">tin m pm Mmzarala, (1910) l.L.R. 33 Mad. 93, 97; 10 M.L.J. 591 (case under <a href=\"/doc/138097064/\" id=\"a_714\">Court Fees\nAct</a>, construing <a href=\"/doc/1873176/\" id=\"a_715\">section 23</a>. Probate and Admn. Act). &#x27;\n\n9(0) Utram Devi v. Dina Nath, 5 PR. 1919, 51 I.C. 651. 652, distinguishing in re Desu Manavalu,\nl.L.R. 33 Mad. 93, on the ground that the remarks therein were obftor.\n(bl Ranmgiri v. Govindanunalr. A.l.R. 1924 Rang. 329. 330.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_207\">Recommendation\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_15\">218</span>\n\nSection\n\nto amend section\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_208\">219.\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_16\">196</span>\n\nThe Bombay practice, as is shown by some reported cases&#x27;, is that when\nthe court grants letters of administration, it is not concerned with inquiries\nabout title. But the Bombay cases did not relate to claims by suri-ir0r3v&#x27;ti[;, The\nclaimants (petitioners) had their claim on the basis of succession, and it was\nthe opposite party that put forth a case that the property was joint family property.\nThe court held that it was not concerned with the question whether the property\nwas, or was not, joint family property. It was no part of the duty of the testa-\nmentary Judge to consider the question of title to property.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_209\">The real con\ufb02ict is between the Punjab and .\\/lzulras rulings. as stated\nabove.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_210\">35.6. We are of the opinion that it is desirable to clai&#x27;ily, the position, in\nview of the conflict of decisions on the subject&#x27;-&#x27;. The Punjab view may, on a\nliteral construction of the section, be correct. But if the law is to be clari\ufb01ed, the\nchoice, we think, should be in favour of the Madras view, which is more practical\nand more convenient. We, therefore, recommend that the Madras view should\nbe adopted, by amending section 218 in a suitable manner. This could be done\nby inserting an Explanation to section 218 in the following terms.\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_154\">&quot;lixplwzzztion--;W/zere the property of it Hindu mtzlivtzlccl coptncenury pt_9&#x27;ses\nby surrirtorslzip (0 the sole surviving C0[)(1&#x27;I&#x27;C\u20acli\u20acI&#x27;, the sole surviring c0pm&#x27;ce&#x27;m&#x27;r\nshall, for the [I_urp0SeS of t/ifs section, be deemed to be a persan entitled\nto the estate of the person on whose death the property so passes, and shall\naccordittgly be entitled to apply for letters of azdrnisrimmit,-:2 umler this\nsection&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_211\">35.8. This takes us to section 219. It provides that it the deceased has died\nintestate and was not a person belonging to any of the classes referred to in\nsection 218, those who are connected with him. either by marriage or by con-\nsanguinity, are entitled to obtain letters of administration of his estate and effects\nin the order and according to the rules hereinafter stated, namely :-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_155\">(a) If the deceased has left a widow, administration shall be granted to the\nwidow, unless the court sees cause to exclude her. either on the ground\nof some personal disquali\ufb01cation. or because she has no interest in the\nestate of the deceased.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_212\">There are two illustrations annexed to this clause, as under :-----\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_156\">&quot;(i) The widow is a lunatic or has committed adultery or has been barred\nby her marriage settlement of all interest in her husband&#x27;s estate. There\nis cause for excluding her from the administration.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_157\">(ii) The widow has married again since the decease of her husband. This\nis not good cause for her exclusion.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_213\">(b) Next, it is provided that if the Judge thinks proper, he may associate\nany person or persons with the widow in the administration who would\nbe entitled solely to the administration if there were no widow.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_214\">(C) If there is no widow, or if the Court sees cause to exclude the widow.\nit shall commit the administration to the person or persons who would\nbe bene\ufb01cially entitled to the estate according to the rules for the\ndistribution of an intestate&#x27;s estate.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_215\">Provided that. when the mother of the deceased is one of the class of\npersons so entitled. she shall &#x27;be solely entitled to administration,\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_216\">(d) Those&#x27; who stand in equal degree of kindred to the deceased are equally\nentitled to administration.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_217\">( e) The husband surviving his wife has the same right of administration\nof her estate as the widow has in respect of the estate of her husband.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_218\">&#x27;(a) 00Ch(1l&#x27;aI&#x27;dHI \\&#x27;. Section 222 provides that probate shall be granted only to an executor Section 222.\nappointed by the will. The appointment may be expressed or by necessary impli--\ncation. There are three illustrations to the section, as under 1\n\n&quot;(ii A wills that C be his executor if B will not. B is appointed executor&#x27;\nby implication.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_167\">(ii) A gives a legacy to B and several legacies to other persons, among\nthe rest to his danghter--in-law C&#x27;. anti adds &quot;but should the within--named\nC be not living I do constitute and appoint B my whole and sole executrix&quot;.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_225\">C is appointed executrix by implication.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_226\">(iii) A appoints several persons executors of his will and codicils and his\nnephew residuary legatee, and in another codicil are these. words.~--&quot;I appoint\nmy nephew my residuary legatee to discharge all lawful demands against\nmy will and codicils signed on different dates&quot;. The nephew is appointed an\nexecutor by implication.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_227\">The section heeds no change, not having created any serious controversies.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_228\">35.13. There is one new point requiring consideration. As the law in India SCCUOH 2?-25\nstands at present, a person appointed executor in the will must be given the gggf\ufb01ggitor\ngrant of probate (if he applies for it), irrespective of his personal un\ufb01tness for &#x27;\nthe position&quot;. He can be removed only if, after commencing the administration\nof the a\ufb01airs of the deceased, he commits breach of trust, waste or other default.\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_168\">&quot;5 as 35 .73&quot;, isrrpra.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_169\">=secuon 2(1).\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_17\">198</span>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_170\">This seems to be English law1 also. The question to be considered is whether\nthis position requires modi\ufb01cation in order to deal with certain special aspects.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_171\">hi this context, we may refer to the suggestion that was made by one\nlearned writer&#x27;-&#x27; a few years ago. His suggestion is that while the court should\nordinarily have regard to the wishes of the testator, it should have a judicial\ndiscretion to exclude, from acting as executor, persons who, on the face of it.\nare un\ufb01t even to begin to administer the affairs of the deceased testator. The\ncourt should not have to wait to see whether the persons so un\ufb01t in fact commit\nwaste or breach of trust and then remove them.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_172\">g&quot;.\u00b0\u00a7\u00b0!&#x27;t P\u00b05&#x27;&quot;\u00b0&quot; 35:14. The pros and cons of the matter may be examined. On the one hand,\nrttictsed. _ - - . . . .,\nthe present position can be ]UStl\ufb01C(l by stating that an executor rs a person of\nthe testat0r&#x27;s own choice and the testator would not prefer someone else&#x27;s\njudgment to his own----not even a judgment of the court. Apparently, on this\nprinciple, the law allows a testator to make the appointment of any person whom\nhe likes as an executor and leaves the question of determining the \ufb01tness of that\nperson absolutely to the choice of the testator.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_173\">As against this, one has to bear in. mind the serious hardship which may\npossibly be caused if a person acts as an executor when he is not \ufb01t at all, and\nwhen (from the known facts), one can infer that the testator would not have\ncontinued the appointment. A situation may, for example, arise where there\nis some interval between the date of the will by which the appointment was\nmade and the death of the testator. The person appointed as executor in the will\n\n5 might have become un\ufb01t after the will, the testator might have not been able to\n\n apply his mind to the question whether the appointment should be continued. The\ntestator might even have mentally taken a decision to make another appointment,\nbut might have found no time to effectuate it in a formal writing.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_174\">In a comment (on -our Working Paper), forwarded to us&quot; by the Catholic\nBishops&#x27; Conference of India, a query has been raised as to how the court will\nform the opinion that a person is &quot;de\ufb01nitely&quot; un\ufb01t to work as executor. It has\nbeen stated, that the action of the court would be arbitrary. We do not, however.\nshare this apprehension. No doubt, positive acts of past misconduct on the part\nof a person would be the best proof of his un\ufb01tness, but one need not rule out\nother situations where adequate material is placed before the court about the\nun\ufb01tness of a person to act as an executor. The court can be expected to act\nwith judgment and discretion. and not arbitrarily, in all such matters.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_175\">)wer to be given. 35.15. Having regard to all aspects of the matter, we are of the opinion\nthat it would be in the \ufb01tness of things if the court is given a power to exclude\nan executor who is de\ufb01nitely unfit. Evidence of actual commission of waste or\nbreach of trust by an executor after taking charge as t.&#x27;,\\&#x27;(&#x27;cut0r, should not be\ninsisted on_ in our opinion,\n\n:commendation to 35.16. In the light of the above discussion, we recommend that the following\n5;: new Section new section should be inserted on the subject-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_176\">&quot;222A. The court may by order exclude from acting as exazcutor, a&#x27; person\nwho, in the opinion of the court, is de\ufb01nitely un\ufb01t to adnzini.stter the af_fait&#x27;s\nof the deceased, whether or not that person has committed waste or breach\nof trust after c-Ommencing administration of such a\ufb02airs :\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_177\">Provided that no such order shall be passed without a\ufb01o.&#x27;-~(./r&#x27;ng such person\na reasonable opportunity of being hear .&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_229\">etion 223 - Pro- 35.17. Some di\ufb02iculty is created by the word &quot;a.ss0ciation&quot;, in relation to\nnon-commercial corporations. For example, does this word cover a University?\nThis question arose before the Allahabad High Court a few years ago. The High\nCourt held that the Banaras University was a &quot;corporation&quot;, but was not an\n&quot;association&quot;, because a corporation having a separate identity from its members\ncannot be called a mere &quot;association&quot; of individuals 4-&#x27;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_235\">The Allahabad High Court also cited an English case&quot;, where James L. J.\nhad regarded &quot;company&quot; and &quot;association&quot; as synonymous, The Allahabad\ncase actually related to section 236, but is equally applicable to section 223, the\nwording in both sections (so far as is material) being the same.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_236\">A similar vicw seems to have been taken by the Oudh&#x27; Chief Court, and\n(impliedly), by the Calcutta High Court&quot;:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_178\">35.19. it would appear that in England, the present law on the subject is\nsomewhat more liberal. Under the statutory provisions now in force in England\nprobate can be granted to a trust corporationi, but judicial decisions and practice\ndirections (or rules of court) permit a corporation sale also to be an executor&#x27;.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_237\">and further permit the grant of probate to a corporation aggregate which is not\na trust corporation. In the latter case, however (i.e. in the case of :1 corporation\naggregate which is not a trust corporation), the probate must be granted to its\nnominee. A registered society is an &quot;association&quot;, but\nnot a &quot;company&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_239\">35.20. As regards other\ncorporate bodies, it is enough if the corporate body applying for probate is\ncompetent to accept probate in law----a question primarily to be decided with\nreference to the law governing its consitution.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_240\">3S.20A. The relevant paragraph in the letter is some-\nwhat un-intelligible, but this seems to be its purport. In this connection, we may\npoint out that the law can merely provide for the capacity of corporations as\nrecognised in the legal system. Internal formalities, such as rules, will, of course,\nhave to be observed by the concerned agencies or authorities within the Church,\nassuming that a particular Church is recognised as a corporation in law.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_241\">35.21. According to section 224, when several executors are appointed.\nprobate may be granted to them simultaneously or at different times.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_245\">The section needs no change.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_246\">35.23. Section 225 provides that if a codicil is discovered after the grant of\nprobate, a separate probate of that codicil may be granted to the executor, if it\nin no way repeals the appointment of executors made by the will. It also provides\nthat if different executors are appointed by the codicil, the probate of the will\nshall be revoked and a new probate granted of the will and the codicil together.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_247\">No amendment is required in the section. which has raised no problems.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_248\">35.24. According to section 226, when probate has been granted to several\nexecutors, and one of them dies, the entire representation of the testator accrues\nto the surviving executor or executors. No amendment is required in this section\n\nalso.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_249\">35.25. Section 227 provides that probate of a will, when granted, establishes\nthe will from the death of the testator, and renders valid all intermediate acts of\nthe executor as such.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_250\">This section also needs no change.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_251\">35.26. Section 228 provides that when a will has been proved and deposited\nin a court of competent jurisdiction situated beyond the limits of the State, whether\nwithin or beyond the limits of India, and a properly authenticated copy of the\nwlil is produced, letters of administration may be granted with a copy&#x27; of such.\nwill annexed.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_252\">Certain questions arise under this section, which concern section 241 also.\nSection 241 is as follows :--\n</p>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_21\">1</span>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_194\">&quot;241. When any cxcurmr is absent from the State in which application\nis made, and there is no executor within the State willing to act, letters of\nadministration, with the will a.nne&#x27;.ued?, may be granted to the attorney or\nagent of the absent executor, for the use and bene\ufb01t of his principal, limited\nuntil he shall obtain probate or letters of administration granted to him.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_253\">35.27. The question that needs to be considered regarding the precise scope\nthis. When a will has been proved and deposited in a\ncourt of competent jurisdiction situated beyond the limits of the State, which\nis the provision whereunder letters of administration (with the will annexed or\nwith a copy of the authenticated copy of the will annexed), can be granted to the\nattorney of the absent executor? It may be noted that section 241 postulates\nproduction of the original will-----note the words &#x27;with the will annexed&quot;. In contrast.\nsection 228 speaks of a copy of the will being annexed. &#x27;\n\n35.28. It is necessary to refer to two cases---one decided by the Allahabad\nHigh Court and the other by the Madras High Court--dealing with the question.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_254\">In the Allahabad case&#x27;, a person had died in Scotland leaving property both\nin India and in the U. K\u00bb. By his will, he appointed an executor, who, in due\ncourse, obtained &#x27;con\ufb01rmation&#x27; of the will from the Court of Scotland. The executor\nthen appointed the present applicant as his attorney for the purpose of apply-\ning to the proper court in India for letters of administration with an authenti--\ncated copy of the will annexed. The attorney accordingly applied to the High\n\nCourt of Allahabad.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_255\">&#x27;Note the word &#x27;copy&#x27;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_256\">&#x27;-&#x27;Note the word &#x27;will&#x27;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_257\">3Para 35 .26, supra.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_258\">&#x27;In re AdwaitNa!h, A.I.R. 1948 All. 351 (F.B.).\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_259\">Qlll\n\nThe High Court noted that the practice in Madras, Calcutta and &#x27;Bombay\nhad been to grant the letters of administration in such cases under section 241,\nbut observed that, &quot;the provisions of section 241, were not intended to apply.\nnor are they appropriate, to the case in which a grant of pi-abate has alirzxrly been\n0l)ftilll(&#x27;\u00a2&quot;l in another court. The section applicable in such case is section 228&#x27;.\nThe High Court took the view that it may, under section 228. grant letters of\nadministration, with a copy ol:&#x27; the authenticated copy of the will annexed. to\nthe agent or attorney of the executor.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_260\">35.29. In the Madras case&#x27;, a lady had died in Eiiglanil, leaviiig a will. By\nthe will she appointed her two nieces to be the executrixes. They obtained probate\nin England and appointed the Lloyds Bank Ltd., Calcutta, as attorneys, who.\nin turn, appointed one of their 0lllC6l&#x27;S, as Attorney, to obtain letters of administra-\ntion with a copy of the will annexed, in respect of the property of the deceased\nin India. The officer of the Lloyds Bank \ufb01led a petition in the Madras High Court\nfor the grant of letters of administration, to have effect only in the State of\nMadras, along with a certi\ufb01ed copy of the probate.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_261\">The question for consideration before the High Court was whether the grant\nwas to be a grant of letters of adniinistratioii under section 241 01&#x27; under section\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_262\">228. The question was material, because the petitioner was unable to produce the\nwill in orighial. The Court observed that section 241 did not require the production\nof the original will. Since the will had been proved and depositcrl in a court of\ncompetent jurisdiction in England, what can be produced here is only a properly\nauthenticated copy of the will. Therefore, a case coming under section 228 would\nbe an exception to the general rule&quot; in section 276 as to the production of the\nwill in origitzal, though the exception is not mentioned in section 276.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_263\">The court said that in section 241, the expression &quot;letters of adniinistration\nwith the will annexed&#x27; had been used in antithesis to letters of administration\ngranted on intestacy. It was permissible to read section 241 along with section\n228, and an attorney or agent of the executor can obtain letters of administration\nwithout producing the original will, if the will had been proved and deposited in\na competent court and a properly authenticated copy of the will is produced.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_264\">35.30. The points of difference between the Allahabad and the Madras\nview can he thus summarised :\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_195\">(a) According to the Allahabad view, in a case under section 241 the will\nmust be established. Not so, according to the Madras High Court:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_196\">(b) While the Allahabad view is that section.228 does not make provision\nfor the grant of administration to an agent or attorney, according to the Madras\nHigh Court. this defect can be cured with the aid of section 24l.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_197\">(c) The Madras High Court has put the case coming under section 228 as\nan exception to the rule provided in section 276. The Allahzibacl High Court has\nrcfiised to accept this argument.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_265\">The controversy has a practical aspect also, concerned with the furnishing\nof a bond. In the kind of cases mentioned above, when the letters of administration\nwere granted under section 228 by the Allahabad High Court, the petitioner had\nbeen asked to furnish an administration bond as required by section 291(1).\nSince the Madras High Court granted letters of administration under section 241,\nan administration bond under section 291(1) was not required to be furnished.\nThe Madras High Court, expressing its disagreement with the above-mentioned\nAllahabad High Court decision, observed, &quot;Sections 228 and 241 should not be\nread as if they provided _for separate &#x27;circumstances and they were mutually\nexclusive. On the other hand, they could be read together. lt -is true that the\nobjects of these two sections differ. The real object of section 228 is to dispense\nwith the production of the proof thereof, for the reason that it had already been\nproved and had been deposited in a court of competent jurisdiction. The real\n&#x27;In re L.C. [.evar/r, A.l.R. 1954 Mad. 898 (PB).\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_266\">2Section 276.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_267\">Points of ditierencc\nbetween Allahabad\nand Madras view\nsummarised.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_268\">Recommendation _\nfor amending sec-- by amending section 241 so as to add&#x27; an Explanation to the effect that (3)\n\ntion\n\nSection 229 to 231.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_269\">Section\n\nUniversal\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_270\">241.\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_271\">232.\n\nand\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_22\">202</span>\n\nobject ol&#x27;_section 241 is to dispense with an application by the executor himself\nwhen he is absent from the province in which the application is made.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_272\">_ According to the Allahabad High Court, these sections are mutually exclu--\nsive and deal with separate circumstances. The Madras High Court, however,\nholds that they are not mutually exclusive and do not provide for separate sets\nof circumstances.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_273\">35.31. in our opinion, it is desirable that the controversy should be resolved\n\nsection 241 will apply also where the case falls under section 228 and (b) if\nthe case falls under section 228. a copy of the will is to sut\ufb01ce and the original\nneed not be produced.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_274\">35.32. Section 229 to 231 deal with renunciation. Although section 230\nis applicable only in the case of an executor, and there is no corresponding pro-\nvision in the act with regard to an administrator, still there is no reason why\nthe principle of these sections should not apply to an administrator. In English\nlaw and practice, there is, as regards the right of renunciation, no distinction made\nbetween the case of an executor and that of an administrator, and the same rule\nshould apply in India.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_275\">As regards the withdrawal of renunciation, the rule is that u renunciation\nby an &#x27;ddmlIl.lSlI&#x27;EitOl&#x27; cannot be withdrawn without the leave of the court, and he\ncan be allowed to withdraw only in a fit and proper case and not merely on\nthe ground that he has changed his mind&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_276\">35.33. Section 232 reads :--~\n\n&quot;232. When----\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_277\">(a) the deceased has made a will, but has not appointed an executor,\nor\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_278\">(b) the deceased has appointed an executor who is legally incapable\nor refuses to act, or who has died before the testator or before he has\nproved the will, or\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_279\">(c) the executor dies after having proved the will, but before he has\nadministered all the estate of the deceased.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_280\">an universal or a residuary legatee may be admitted to prove the will, and\nletters of administration with the will annexed may be granted to him of\nthe whole estate, or of so much thereof as may be unadmimstered&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_281\">35.34. Section 232 is concerned with two classes of legatees, namely the\n\n&#x27;\u00b05&#x27;d&quot;&quot;&#x27;&quot;3&quot; &#x27;\u00b03&quot;&#x27;\u00b0\u00b0S universal legatee and the residuary legatee. The universal legatee is one who, by\n\nNo change needed\nin section\n\nvirtue of the will, is entitled to the whole of the testator&#x27;s property. In the absence\nof the executor, he has been given the right to prove the will and&#x27; to obtain letters\nof administration with the will annexed. He cannot, however, obtain probate.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_282\">The residuary legatee is, it is said, the testator&#x27;s choice; he is the next person\nin his election to the executor3. When the residum has been disposed of to another\nperson, there would be no purpose in giving the administration to the next--of-kin,\nbecause no bene\ufb01t can accrue to them by the grant of the Letters of Administration\n\nin such cases.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_283\">35.35. What words would create a universal or residuary legatee is not\n\n232&#x27; a matter with which we are concerned at the moment. that teing a question of\n\ncor struction of the words of the will. It is enough to note zhat there are adequate\n\nreasons for the grant of letters of Administration to the universal legatee or the .\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_284\">residuary legatee. in preference to the next-of-kin.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_285\">The section, therefore, needs no change.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_286\">7&#x27;4&#x27;l:o be carried out under <a href=\"/doc/1149799/\" id=\"a_720\">section 24</a> l \n3The Mancltersa, A.l.R. l929Bom. 33, 34 (Rangnekar 1.).\nAtkins v. Bernard. 2 Phill, C.l8. cited in N.D. Basu. Law of Succession (1957), page 699.\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_287\">35.36. Section 233 provides that when a residuary legatec who has a bene-- Section\n\n\ufb02cial interest survives the testator, but dies before the estate has been fully adminis-\ntered, his representative has the same right to administration with the \\vill annexed\n\nas such residuary legatee.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_288\">The section needs no change,\n\n35.37. According to section 234, when there is no egtecutor and no residuary\nlegatee or representative of a residuary legatee, or he declines or is incapable to\nact, or cannot be found, the person or persons who would be entitled to the\nadministration of the estate of the deceased if he had died intestate, or any other\nlegatee having a bene\ufb01cial interest, or a creditor, may be admitted to prove the\nwill, and letters of administration may be granted to him or them accordingly.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_289\">The section needs no change,\n\n35.38. According to section 235, letters of administration with the will annexed\nshall not be granted to any legatee other than an universal or a residuary legatee,\nuntil a citation has been issued and published in the manner hereinafter mentioned,\ncalling on the next--of-kin to accept or refuse letters of administration.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_290\">The section also needs no change.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_291\">35.39. Section 236 provides as follows 2\n\n&quot;236. Letters of administration cannot be granted to any person who is a\nminor or is of unsound mind, nor to any association of individuals unless it\n\nis a company which satis\ufb01es the conditions prescribed by rules to be made by\nthe State Government in this behalf.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_292\">35.40. In view of what has been stated&#x27; above under section 223. we re-\ncommend that section 236 should be revised as follows :---\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_198\">&quot;236. Letters of administration cannot be granted--~\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_199\">(a) to any person who is a minor or is of unsound mind, or\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_200\">(b) to any company unless it satisfies the conditions prescribed by rules\nto be made by the State Government in this behalf, or\n\n( C) to any corporation other than a company, if it is not legally competent\nto accept letters of administration.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_293\">CHAPTER 36\nLIMITED GRANTS : SECTIONS 237 TO 260\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_294\">233.\nSection 234.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_295\">Section 235.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_296\">Section 236.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_297\">Recommendation.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_298\">36.] Limited grants are dealt with in some detail in sections 237 to 260. Limited grams_\n\nThe various types ol such grants are as follows :---\nI. grants limited in duration--sections 237 to 240;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_299\">II. grants for the use and bene\ufb01t of others having a right--sections 24l---\n246;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_300\">III. grants for special purp0ses------secti0ns 247---254;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_301\">IV. grants with exception---sections 255-256;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_302\">V. grants of the rCst------section 257;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_303\">VI. grants of effects un--administered---sections 258~--260.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_304\">lseediscussion as to section 223, supra.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_305\">85-L/B(D)l44MofLJ&amp;CA-14\n\n\n\nSections 237-238\nIntroductory.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_306\">Loss of will before\n\ndeath--applicability\n\nof section_ 237.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_307\"><a href=\"/doc/1873176/\" id=\"a_721\">Section 23</a> <a href=\"/doc/1968317/\" id=\"a_722\">8-</a>\nNarrow scope.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_308\">Defect in section\n237 as to destruc-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_309\">tion otherwise than\nthe act or mistake\nof testator.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_310\">Testator not men-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_311\">tally competent.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_312\">Want of symmetry\nbetween sections 237\nand 238.\n</p>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_23\">204</span>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_313\">1. Grants limited in duration.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_314\">_ 36.2. Section 237 deals two situations. When a will has been lost or mislaid\nSIl1Ce the testator&#x27;s death, probate may be granted of a copy or draft of the\nwill. This is the \ufb01rst situation dealt with in the section. The second situation\nrelates to the cases where a will, &quot;has been destroyed by wrong or accident and\nnot by any act of the testator&quot;. Here again, a copy or the draft may be utilised\nfor the grant of probate. In both the cases, the probate is limited until the\noriginal Wlll or a properly authenticated copy is produced. __\n\n36.3. According to judicial construction of section 237, the words &quot;since\nthe testator&#x27;s death&quot; qualify only the word &quot;mislaid&quot;, and have no referene to\nthe word &quot;lost&quot;. Thus, according to judicial construction, probate can be granted\nwhere the will is lost before or after the testator&#x27;s death. In our opinion, it is\ndesirable to codify this interpretation so as to make the section self-contained.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_315\">Of course, the general rule: that there is a rebuttable presumption that the\ntestator destroyed the will where the will lost proved to be in his custody\nand is not forthcoming applies in India also. At least, it was so held in a\nCalcutta case?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_316\">-But another case&#x27; seems to create a doubt in this regard, inasmuch as it\nholds that there is no rule of law that under certain circumstances a presumption\nshould be made that the will was revoked. In our opinion, the earlier Calcutta\ncase takes :1 more practical view. and that view should be incorporated in the\n\nsection&#x27;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_317\">36.4. Under section 238, probate can be granted if the will has been lost\nor destroyed and no copy has been made or draft preserved, Probate is then\ngranted of the contents of the will &quot;if, they (the contents) can be established by\nevidence&quot;. &quot;Evidence&quot; here, of course, means secondary evidence. The topic was\ndiscussed in detail in the leading English case of Sugden v. Lord St. Leonw-ds&#x27;,\nwhere 21. will was established by parol evidence alone.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_318\">36.5. Reverting to section 237, it -would appear that section 237 is narrowly\ndrawn. The words &#x27;wrong or accident&#x27; do not, if taken literally, cover the\ncases where a natural calamity, such as a \ufb02ood, destroys the will. The section\nis ,also defective, inasmuch as the language does not make it clear that the\nsection applies only where the testator had no intention of revoking the will.\nIf an act of the testator done with the intention of revoking the will result in\n&quot;destruction of the will&quot;, it is obvious that the section should not be applicable.\nThis situation should be exeulded from the scope of the section?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_319\">36.6 On the other hand, where, though the will is destroyedl by the\nphysical act of the testator, the testator is not a person in possession of his\nmental faculties, the situation should be included in the section. We are recom-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_320\">mending to cover these points&quot;.\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_201\">&quot;Ibis criticism applies, in substance, to section 238 also. ,\n\n36.7. With reference to section 238, it is further to be pointed out that\nit does not deal with the various situations so elaborately as the preceding\nsection---section 237----does. Grants for the bene\ufb01t of others\n\n36.13. Section 241 reads--~\n\n&quot;When any executor is absent from the State in which application is made,\nand there is no executor within the State willing to act, letters of &quot;administration,\nwith the will annexed, may be granted to the attorney or agent of the absent\nexecutor, for the use and bene\ufb01t of his principal, limited until he shall obtain\nprobate or letters of administration granted to himself.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_321\">In connection with this section, certain question Ltriseby reason of secton\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_322\">273. Section 273 reads----\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_225\">&quot;273. Probate or letters of administration shall have etlect over all the\nproperty and estate. movable or immovable, of the deceased, throughout\nthe State in which the same is or are granted and &#x27;shall be conclusive as\nto the representative title against all debtors of the deceased and all persons\nholding property which belongs to him, and shall afford full, indemnity\nto all debtors, paying their debts and all persons delivering up such pro-\nperty to the person to whom such probate or letters of administration have\nbeen granted: ~\n\nProvided that probates and letters of administration granted----\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_226\">(a) by a High Court, or\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_227\">(b) by a District Judge, where the deceased at the time of his death\nhad a \ufb01xed place of abode situate within the jurisdiction of such\nJudge, and such Judge certi\ufb01es that the value of the property and\n,estate affected beyond the limits of the State does not exceed ten\nthousand rupees.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_323\">shall, unless otherwise directed by the grant, have like effect throughout the\nother States&quot;.\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_228\">&quot;The proviso to this section shall apply in India after the separation of\nBurma and Aden from India to probates and letters of administration\ngranted in Burma and Aden before the date of the separation, or after\nthat date in proceedings which were pending at that date.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_229\">&quot;The proviso shall also apply in India after the separation of Pakistan from\nIndia to probates and letters of administration granted before the date of\nthe separation, or after that date in proceedings pending at that date, in\nany of the territories which on that date constituted Pakistan.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_324\">36.14. It should be noted that section 273 empowers the court (in certain\ncases) to make a grant elfective beyond the lmms of the State. In order that\nthe full bene\ufb01t of this wider jurisdiction of the court under section 273 may be\navailable, it is desirable that section 241 should also be relaxed, We, therefore,\nrecommend that suitable words excluding from section 241, the case, where an\norder is passed under stction 273 proviso should be added.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_325\">36.15. Thrc is some obscurity on the question whether the &quot;attorney&quot;\n(agent) should be a resident of the State. It appears, however, that the attorney\nmay reside anywhere in India, if the grant is by the High Court.-&#x27; This interpreta-\ntion can be suitably codi\ufb01ed.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_326\">36.16. In the light of the above discussion we recommend that section 241\nshould be revised as below;\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_230\">&quot;Z41, When any executor is absent from the State in which application is\nmade, and there is no executor within the State willing to act letters of\nadministration. with the will annexed may be granted to the attorney or\nagent of the absent executor, for the use and bene\ufb01t of his principal, limited\nuntil he shall obtain probate or letters of administration granted to himself.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_231\">1In7hego0rIsofCartrl,v, 28 PR. 1883.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_26\">207</span>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_232\">/irception: Nutlting in this section applies to (1 case falling within the\nproviso to section 273.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_233\">[Exploitation : ll&quot;/zere the grant is by the High Court, it is not necessary that\nthe attorney or agent should be residing within the State, provided he\nresides within India.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_327\">36.17. Section 242 provides as follows :--~ Section 242.\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_234\">&quot;242. When any person to whom if present, letters of administration, with\nthe will annexed, might be granted is &#x27;absent from the state, letters of adminis-\ntration, with the will annexed, may be granted to his attorney or agent,\nlimited as mentioned in <a href=\"/doc/1149799/\" id=\"a_725\">section 24</a>] \n\nThe section needs no change.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_235\">36.18. Section 243 reads----- Section 243-\n&quot;243. When a person entitled to administration in case of intestacy is\nabsent from the State and no person equally entitled is willing to act, letters\nof administration may be granted to the attorney or agent of the absent\nperson, limited as mentioned in section 24l.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_328\">The section needs no change.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_329\">36.19. Section 244 reads as follows :-- Section 244.\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_236\">&quot;244. When a minor is sole executor or 5010, residuary legatee, letters of\nadministration with the will annexed may be granted to the legal guardian\nor such minor or to such other person as the Court may think \ufb01t until\nthe minor has attained his majority, at which period, and not before,\nprobate of the will shall be granted to him.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_330\">It has been held that the expression &quot;legal guardian&quot;, in this section, means\na guardian appointed under the <a href=\"/doc/1874830/\" id=\"a_726\">Guardians and Wards Act, 1980</a>. If the natural\nguardian wants to apply under this section, he must first take proceedings (under\nthe <a href=\"/doc/1874830/\" id=\"a_727\">Guardians and Wards Act</a>) to be appointed legal guardian.&#x27; If there is a\nlegal guardian of the person of the minor and another of the property of the\nminor, the latter is entitled to the grant&quot; (compare section 246, where the words\n&quot;care&#x27;of his estate&quot; are used).\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_331\">A guardian appointed by the father under <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_728\">section 69</a> of the Succession Act\nwould appear to be a &quot;legal guardian&quot; within the meaning of this section.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_332\">It appears desirable to codify the law on the subject, as laid down in the\nabove decisions.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_333\">36.20. In the light of the above, the following Explanation should be Recommendation to\ninserted below section 244 :--- amend 5\u00b0&#x27;-&#x27;&quot;\u00b0&quot; 244-\nExplanationz In this section, &quot;legal guardian&quot; means a guardian of the\n\nproperty appointed by a person or authority having power to do so, whether\nwithin or outside India, but does not include a natural guardian.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_334\">36.21. Section 245 deals with administration during the minority of Section 245--\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_335\">Several executors or several residuary legatees. An administration during R\u00b0\u00b0\u00b0&quot;&quot;&quot;\u00b0&quot;d&#x27;&quot;i\u00b0&quot;&#x27;\n\nminority is, under the section, limited until one of the executors attains majority.\nIt is obvious that the section deals with the case dealt with in section 244 and,\nis indeed, a kind of Explanation to that section. This should be brought out\nby adding, after the words &quot;the grant&quot;, the words and \ufb01gures &quot;under section 244&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_336\">We recommend that section 245 should be amended accordingly.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_337\">36.22. Section 246 reads as follows :-- A Section 246.\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_237\">&quot;246. If a sole executor or a sole universal or residuary legatee, or a person\nwho would be solely entitled to the estate of the intestate according to the\n\n&#x27;(a) Arumilli Virammrz v. Arumilli\ufb02Seshamma, 1931 Mad 343, \n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_238\">(b) In the goods of Nirojini Debi, (1907) I.L.R. 34 Cal. 706.\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_338\"><span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"1378256\" id=\"span_27\"><a href=\"/doc/1378256/\" id=\"a_729\">&#x27;Ganjasur Koer V. The Collector of Patna, I.L.R.</a> 25 Cal. 795.</span>\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_339\">Overlapping with\nsection 244.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_340\">Recommendation\n\nto revise section246.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_341\">Section 247\u00bb \u00ab\nAdministration\npendente lite.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_342\">Section 247 (1)-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_343\">who can apply.\n</p>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_28\">208</span>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_344\">rule for the distribution of intestates&#x27; estate applicable in the case of the\ndeceased. is a minor or lunatic, letters of administration, with or without\nthe will annexed, as the case may be shall be granted to the person to whom\nthe care of his estate has been committed by competent authority, or, it&#x27;\nthere is no such person, to such other person as the Court may think fit to\nappoint, for the use and bene\ufb01t of the minor or lunatic until he attains\nm(t_i0riI_r or lwmnzcs of sozmd mind, as the case may be&quot;. ,\n\n36.23. There is some overlapping between this section and section 244,\nas regards a minor sole executor or a minor sole residuary legatee. In <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_730\">section\n217</a> of the Succession Act of 1865 (corresponding to present section 246), the\nword &#x27;minor&#x27; did not occur, but the word -occured in <a href=\"/doc/184967347/\" id=\"a_731\">section 33</a> of the Probate\nand Administration Act. The effect wasthat as regards persons governed by the\n<a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_732\">Succession Act</a> of 1865 in the case of intestacy, if the minor was solely entitled\nto the estate of the intestate, there was no provision for the grant of letters of\nadministration to his guardian on his behalf. If the minor was the sole executor\nor sole universal or residuary legatee, grant could be made under section 215\n(present section 244). The insertion of the word &quot;minor&quot; in section 246 (in\nl925) was intended to supply this omission. But it has created overlapping&#x27;\nso far as the minor sole&#x27; executor or minor sale residuary legatee is concerned, as\nsection 244 has already provided for this contingency. So far, therefore, as sec-\ntion 246 is concerned the word &quot;minor&quot; should be read as con\ufb01ned to the\ncase of intestacy.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_345\">36.24. In our opinion, it is desirable to amend section 246 to bring out\nthat point.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_346\">To carry out the abo\\&#x27;c object. we recommend that section 246 should be\nrevised as follows :\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_239\">&quot;246, lf~--4\nta) a sole cit-cuttn&#x27; or a sole universal or residuary legatee is a\nlunatic. or\n\n(I7) a pcl&#x27;s(&#x27;El \\\\/lzo would be solely entitled to the estate of the intestate,\naccording to the rule for the distribution of intestates&#x27; estate applicable\nin the ease of the (leceascd, is a minor or a lunatic,\n\nletters of adinir&quot;sttation, with or without the will annexed as the case\nmay be, shall lk granted to the. person to whom the care of his estate\nhas been committed by competent authority, or, if there is no such person,\nto such other person as the Court may think \ufb01t to appoint, for the use\nand benefit of the minor or lunatic until he attains majority or becomes of\nsound mind. as the Case may be&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_347\">llll. (Sran-ts for special purposes.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_348\">36.25. Ac&#x27;/m1&#x27;nistratimt pemlente lite is dealt with in section 247, which\nreads as follows :----~\n&quot;247. Pending any suit touching the validity of the will of a deceased or for\nobtaining or revoking any probate or any grant of letters of administration.\nthe Court may appoint an administrator of the estate of such deceased\nperson. who shall have all the rights and powers of a general administra-\ntor, other than the right of distributing such estate, and every such adminis-\ntrator shall be subject to the immediate control of the court and shall act\nunder its direction&quot;. -\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_349\">36.26. A question that requires to be considered is--Who can make an\napplication under this section ? In a Calcutta case&#x27;, it was held that an applcation\nunder section 247 can be made only by the parties to the suit. According to the\n\n&#x27;See Paruck, Sticcession&#x27;Act,&quot;Comrnentary on section 246.\n2Nirod \\&#x27;. C/1amtkarz&#x27;m&#x27;, (I392) 19 CW.N. 205.\n</p>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_29\">209</span>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_350\">English practice, however, such an application may be made by any person\ninterested in the estate, and even by a creditor of the deceased.&#x27;\n\n36.27. The appointment: of an administrator pendente lite does not follow Appointment-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_351\">as a matter of course whenever litigation is pending. Before granting administra-\ntion pcndrzle lite, the Court must be satis\ufb01ed as to the necessity of such _&#x27;an\nadministrator. The applicant is required to show, for instance, that it is neces-\nsary fon the preservation of the estate, for receiving rents, interest or dividends on\nshares, and that no \ufb01t and proper person is in a position to discharge these\noffices&quot;. \u00bb\n\n36.28. Having considered the present position, we are of the view that it is Recommendation\ndesirable to amend the section so as to provide that an application for the to amend Section\nappointment of an administrator under the section can be made by any person 247-\ninterested in the estate, and even by a creditor of the deceased. Such a widening\nof the section is likely to increase the utility of the section, in cases where the\nparties to the suit do not take any initiative\ufb02 An Explanation to the above\neffect can be added. We recommend accordingly.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_352\">36.29. Section 255 reads--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_249\">&quot;255. Whenever the nature of the case requires that an exception be made,\nprobate of a will. or letters of administration with the will annexed, shall\nbe granted subject to such exception.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_377\">36.37. Section 256 provides that whenever the nature of the case requires that\nan exception be made, letters of administration shall be granted subject to such\nexception\n\n36.38. An important point concerning sections 255-256 arises out of a\nMadras case.&#x27; In that case, the appellant, the widow of one S.R., had applied for\ngrant of letters of administration in respect of a bank account and death bene\ufb01t\npayable by a company (apparently, the employer of the deceased). S.R. had\ndied at Madras, leaving a will dated 14~l-1964. He had an account in the United\nCommercial Bank jointly with the appellant (the wife) and her daughter. The\nrespondent claimed to be the senior wife of the deceased and contended that the\nassets left by the deceased amounted to much more than the value mentioned in the\naffidavit of assets. She contended that it was necessary that all the assets of the\ndeceased should be valued and brought into the a\ufb01idavit of assest of the deceased,\nfor granting letters of administration.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_378\">. 1HapzInab Annat/mi Rangqch(rr1&#x27; v. R. A/rcznt/talakslznzi Rangaclzari,  1975 A:Madrasv&#x27;_i42\n(Veeraswami CJ. and Natarajan J.).\n</p>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_31\">211</span>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_379\">The High Court, dealing with this contention, first noted that the general\nrule is that the entire assets should be disclosed in the al\ufb01davit of assets&#x27;, which\nwill c-ount for \\&#x27;aluati0n for the purposes of court fees. But where it is a case\nof joint account in 21 bank and the amount is payable to either or survivor, the\nnature of the case requires that it should be treated as an exception to the above\nrule that the entirety of the assets of the deceased should be disclosed in the\na\ufb02idavit of assets. The High Court also held that it might not even be necessary\nto obtain letters of administration. for there was, in this case, little to administer,\nand the wife&#x27;s right to draw the amount could be independently of the will.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_380\">However, since the bank had been insisting on letters of administration,\nthe High Court held that the application should be granted, the case being treated\nas one falling within the &quot;exception&quot; clause in section 255.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_381\">36.39. &#x27;the facts of this case, when examined closely, disclose need for Recommendation\namending the law. It, in such cases, obtaining letters of administration becomes {is &#x27;O 30&quot;&quot; a\u00b0C\u00b0&quot;D*\nnecessary by reason ol the practice of banks, should it not be covered specifically &quot;&#x27;aba--&quot;k&#x27;\nin section 255 (and also in section 256)?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_382\">36.40. As the language of section 255 stahds at present, the proposition that Recommendation\nthe grant can be limited to a particular item is not so evident from the section. 1\u00b0;-&#x27;t&#x27;m\u00a2&quot;d 5\u20ac\u00b0&quot;\u00b0&quot;\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_250\">It appears to be desirable to amend? section 255, so as to make this clear. The &quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_383\">object could he achieved by adding the following exception to section 253 :\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_251\">l:&#x27;,~.c&#x27;eptiot1 to /we atlrlerl to sec&#x27;II&#x27;on 255.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_252\">Exception------Notwithstanding anything cotztuined in this section or in\nsection 256, where moneys have been deposited in joint account in a bank\nand the cmmmir thereof is payabie to either or any survivor of the persons\nctepositing the money, the application for letters of adrninistration in respect\nof. those m0ne_\\&#x27;.r may, on the death of either or any of touch persons, be\nmade by the sm&#x27;\\~.&quot;vor.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_253\">V. GRANIS : SECTIONS 261--263\n37.1. Alteration and rcxocafon of grants form the subject matter of sections\n\n261 to 263, which do not need any introductory comments.\n_ 37.2. Section.2ol provides that errors in names and descriptions, or in\nsetting forth the time and place of the deceased&#x27;s death, or the purpose in a\n\nlimited grant, may be rectified by the Court, and the grant of probate or letters of\nadinmistration may be altered and amended accordingly,\n\nThe section needs no change.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_396\">37.3. Section 262 provides that if, after the grant of administration with the\nWlll annexed, a codicil is discovered, it may be added to the grant on due proof\nand identi\ufb01cation. an(l the grant may be altered and amended accordingly.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_397\">The section needs no change.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_398\">374. Under section 263 the grant of-probate or letters of administration\nmay be revoked or annulled for Just. cause. The Explanation provides that just\ncause shall be deemed to exist where----\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_399\">(a) the proceedings to obtain the grant were defective in substance; or\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_400\">(b) the grant was obtained fraudulently by making a false suggestion or\nby concealing from the Court something material to the case; or\n\nthe grant was obtained by means of an untrue allegation of a fact\nessential in point of law to justify the grant, though such allegation\nwas made in ignorance or inadvertently; or &#x27;\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_401\">(c)\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_402\">(d) the grant has become useless and inoperative through circumstances; or\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_403\">(e) the person to whom the grant was made has wilfully and without reason-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_404\">able cause omitted to exhibit an inventory or account in accordance with\nthe provisions of Chapter Vll of this Part, or has exhibited under that\nChapter an inventory or account which is untrue in a material respect.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_405\">The\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_406\">(i)\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_407\">(ii)\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_408\">(iii)\n</p><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_409\">(iv)\n\nfollowing illustrations are appended:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_254\">the Court by which the grant was made had no jurisdiction;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_255\">the grant was made without citing parties who Ought to have been cited:\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_256\">the will of which probate was obtained was forged or revoked;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_257\">A,&#x27;obtained letters of administration to the estate of B, as his widow.\nbut it has since transpired that she was never married to him;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_258\">A has taken administration to the estate of B, as if he had died intestate,\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_259\">(v)\nbut :1 will has since been discovered;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_260\">(vi) since probate was granted, a later will has been discovered;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_261\">(vii) since probate was granted, a codicil has been discovered which revok-\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_410\">ed or adds to the appointment of executors under the will;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_411\">(viii) the person to whom probate was, or letters of administration were,\n\ngranted, has subsequently become of unsound mind.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_412\">It may be stated with reference to the second illustration that the absence of\n\ncitation on a person who&#x27; was interested, being a transferee from the heirs at\n\nlaw, would be a defect of substance which is &quot;just cause&quot; within the meaning of\nsection 263. l&#x27;\u20ac&gt;:planation.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_413\">The case may be different, where the parties themselves have led evidence? &#x27;&quot;\n</p>\n<blockquote Section 269 reads----\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_298\">&quot;269(l) Until probate is granted of the will of a deceased person, or an\nadministrator of his estate is constituted, the District Judge, with-&#x27;\nin whose jurisdiction any part of the property of the deceased person is\nsituate, is authorised and required to interfere for the protection of such\nproperty at the instance of any person claiming to be interested therein, and\nin all other cases where the Judge considers that the property incurs any\nrisk of loss or damage; and for that purpose, if he thinks fit. to appoint an\nofficer to take and keep possession of the property.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_299\">Section Q69.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_300\">(2) This section shall not apply when the -deceased is a Hindu, Moham-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_457\">madan, Budhist, Sikh or Jain or an exempted person. nor shall it apply\nto any part of the property of an Indian Christian who has died intestate&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_458\">We have no comments on the section.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_459\">I\n\n38.8. It appears that section 270 would cover a case even where the Person not domici-\ntestator or intestate was a person not domiciled in Indz&#x27;a--say, a foreign national-- led in India-\nwho left property most of which was outside India, while leaving some movable\nproperty in India. &quot;Of course, section 270 is not concerned with the question dealt\n\nwith in <a href=\"/doc/615438/\" id=\"a_736\">section 4</a> and <a href=\"/doc/1290386/\" id=\"a_737\">5</a>,--the law applicable regarding succession. The section is\n\nis&quot;.=;tton 2 (bb): C\n\n\n\nSection 270-\nAssets in India.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_463\">Origin.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_464\">English cases.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_465\">Section 271.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_466\">Section 272-----Pro-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_467\">bate and letters of\nadministration may\nbe granted by\ndelegate.\n</p>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_38\">216</span>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_468\">concerned only with the jurisdiction of lndian courts. it would appear----though\nthere is no case law on this poii1t----that, in so far as a n0n--domiciled person\nhas left property in India, the section would apply. The law applicable will, of\ncourse, be governed by <a href=\"/doc/615438/\" id=\"a_738\">sections 4-</a>-5. .\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_469\">For the present, and, in the\n\nabsence of case law raising any concrete questions.\nwe have no further comments 0\n\nn this point.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_470\">38.10. The foundation of the jurisdiction of a Court to grant probate or\nletters of administration is that there is property of the deceased to be administered\nwith the country and section 270 does not, in any way, provide an alternative\nbasis for a grant&#x27;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_471\">Thus, the section cannot be understood as dispensing w\n\nith the necessity\nof existence of Indian assets before probate, etc. can issue from\n\nan Indian Court.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_472\">38.11. Section 270 has its origin in s\n1857 (20 and 2] Vict. C. 77).\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_473\">ection 46 of the Court of Probate Act.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_474\">38.12. English decisions from the earliest time\nassets as a condition precedent to an English grant. F\nwell held!\u00bb-\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_475\">have insisted on English\nor example Sir C. Cress-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_301\">&quot;It does not appear from the affidavits that the deceased left any property\nin this country. Unless he did so, there is nothing upon which the grant\nasked for would operate and I should have no jurisdiction to decree letters\nof administration to be granted&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_476\">Sir J.P. Wilde observed in another case5 2\n\n&quot;It is not one of the functions of this Court to determine as an abstract\nqustion who is the proper representative of a deceased person. The\nfoundation of the jurisdiction of this Court is, that there is personal pro~\nperty of the deceased to be distributed within its jurisdiction. In this case\nthe deceased has no property, within this country, and the Court has,\ntherefore, no jurisdiction&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_477\">Other decisions to the same effect are &#x27;In Goods of Pittock&#x27; where, administra-\ntion was refused because the deceased left no personal pr_ope1&#x27;ty in England and----\nIn Goods of Coode&#x27; where it was held that a will disposing only of property\nin a foreign country was not entitled to an English probate.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_478\">It should, however, be\nthe existence of assets in In\none is enough.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_479\">pointed out that what section 270 requires is not\ndia in every case. The &quot;interest&quot; in the estate, mentioned in section 283(l)(c), is only\n\nthat interest which, by citation, a person called upon may claim to have in the\nestate of the deceased. It is not the interest which the deceased did not own,\nbut which the claimant, coniing into the picture by citation, claims to be vested\niii himself .The title of the testator to the property which is the subject-matter\nof disposition is entirely and necessarily outside the scope of probate proceedings;\nthat question will have to be settled separately. By citation, a party cannot be\nallowed to convert a probate proceeding into a suit for resolving title. This is the\ngist of a Madras jtitlgineim\n\nThe section needs no cliunge.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_514\">38.27.
