Section 291 reads as under------ &#x27; semen 291_\n&quot;291. (1) Every person to whom any grant of letters of administration, &#x27;,\u00a7,&quot;,&#x27;,&quot;,;&#x27;_&quot;&quot;&quot;&quot;&#x27;\u00b0&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_530\">other than a grant under section 241, is committed shall give a bond to\nthe District Judge with one or more surety or surities, engaging for the due\ncollection, getting in, _and administering the estate of the deceased, which\nbond shall be in such form as the Judge may, by general or special order,\n\ndirect.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_531\">(2) When the deceased was a Hindu, Muhammadan, Bhudhist, Sikh or\nJaina or an exempted person--\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_532\">(a) the exception made by sub-section (1) in respect of a grant under\nsection 241 shall not operate;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_533\">(b) the District Judge may demand a like bond from any person\nto whom probate is granted&quot;. _\n\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_45\">222</span>\n\n_ Need for giving 38.35. As the section stands at present, there is no discretion given to\nd&#x27;5\u00b0&quot;\u00b0&quot;\u00b0&quot; l\u00b0 \u00b0&#x27;&quot;&quot;&quot; the court to dispense with the taking of the bond. If sub-section (1) applies.\nthen the only matter in respect of which the court has a discretion is as regards\nthe amount of the bond. Now. cases do arise in practice where a bond might\nbecome an idle forma1ity--for example, where the applicant for the grant of\nletters of administration is the sole legatee or the sole heir. Under the present\nsection, even in the case of a sole legatee, the only matter in the direction of the\ncourt is the amount of the bond.&#x27; But subject to the above discretion and apart\nfrom cases speci\ufb01cally execluded by statute,&quot; the provision as to security in sub-\nsection (1) is mandatory.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_534\">Rationale for taking 38.36. Security is required for guarding against possible malpractices by the\n\nSGCUTW b\u00b0&quot;d- grantee. If there is reasonable protection against a malpractice arising front\nthe special circumstances of the case, the taking on an administration bond is an\nunnecessary formality. It may even cause hardship. For example, the conditions\nimposed or the amount \ufb01xed may make it impracticable for the applicant to\nfumish the security demanded and consequently no letters of administration can\nbe taken out. In such circumstances the safety of the estate may be seriously\nimperilled if letters of administration are not granted?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_535\">38.37. It may be noted that in England, while a bond is generally taken\nfrom an administrator, the court has a discretion to dispense with sureties by\nreason of special circumstances. For example, where all the debts had been paid\nand the persons applying were entitled to the whole of the residue, sureties\nwere dispensed with.&#x27; Againf where the applicant had no means, surities were\ndispensed with, to enable the applicant (widow of the deceased) to carry on the\nbusiness of the deceased.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_536\">Englishlaw.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_537\">Need of giving dis- 38.38. On u catclui et.ttsittct&#x27;atioi1, of the matter, it seems to us that the\n\u00b01&#x27;\u00b0&quot;\u00b0&quot; t\u00b0&#x27;h\u00b0 \u00b0\u00b0&#x27;&quot;&quot; Court should be given a discretion to dispense with the taking of the administra-\ntion bond or with the need for sureties in the following cases :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_350\">(i) where the person to whom the grant is to be made is&quot; a sole legat&#x27;ee.=\n\nor Z! sole heir. or\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_351\">(ii) where, for reasons to be recorded, the Court in the circumstance of\nthe case thinks it proper to dispense with the bond or sureties.&#x27;\n\nHistory of 5ut,.5,,c- 38,39. As to sub-section (2) of section 291, its history is of interest. In\ntion (2). the case of probate, a bond can he demanded only from the special classes of\npersons mentioned in sub--scction (2). The reason for the difference is thus stated\nin the Statement&#x27; of Objects and Reasons of the Probate and Administration\nAct, 1881: &quot;The Indian Succession Act, 1865 provided for the taking of security\nfor the due discharge of this office only from an administrator, it being considered\nthat in the case of an executor, who was selected by the testator himself, such\nsecurity can safely be dispensed with. But amongst the classes to which this\nAct will apply, cases will, it is apprehended, accasionally occur, in which it may be\n\nexpedient to take security even from an executor and accordingly a secti0n has\n\nbeen inserted in the Bill amending section 256.of the seccession Act- in such a\nmanner as to give a power to the court to require an executor to give security.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_538\">Recommendation 38.40. The special topic of &quot;executors of their wrong&quot; is dealt with in a\nseparate Chapter--sections 303 and 304. Of these two sections, one defines the\nconcept, and the other deals with the liability, of such an executor.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_573\">W1I&#x27;.}a\u00a7i}7Z\u00a3I1vfivizikzinder Kmnal:/~A.I.R. 1973 Raj 233. &#x27;\n\n\n\n.412\u00bb\n\n39.2. Section 303 provides that a person who interineddles with the estate \u00a7~&quot;-W0&quot; f3U3}&#x27;,&#x27;\nof the deceased, or does any Other act which belongs to the otiice of executor. n5:&#x27;]\u00b0V\u00b0V&#x27;::)&#x27;\u00a7g.._\u00b0 &quot;5\nwhile the_re is no rightful executor or administrator in existence. thereby makes\nhimself an executor of his own wrong.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_574\">There are, however, two exceptions to this general provision:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_360\">&lt;1) lntermeddling with the goods ol&#x27; the deceased for the purpose oi&#x27; pre-\nserving them or providing for his funeral or for the immediate neces-\nsities of his lamily or property. does not make an executor of his own\nwrong.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_361\">(3) Dealing in the ordiiiury course of business with goods&#x27; of the deceased\nreceived from another does not make an executor of his own wrong.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_575\">illustration ti) L&#x27;.\\pl&#x27;c1lllS the scope of the inain as follows 2--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_362\">&quot;(l) A uses or gives away or sells some of the goods of the deceased, or\ntakes them to satisfy his own debt or legacy or receives payment of the\ndebts of the deceased. He is an executor of his own wrong.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_576\">However, if there is no notice of death. there is no intcrmeddling: Thus, in\nillustration (ii), A, having been appointed agent by the deceased in his lifetime\nto collect his debts and sell his goods, continues to do so after he has become\naware of his death. He is an E&#x27;}&#x27;.CCLll0l&#x27; of his own wrong in respect of acts done\nafter he has become aware of the death of the deceased.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_577\">The section does not seem to need any change\n\n&#x27;1 V _ , - l  . . . , . _ Section 304--\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_578\">39.3. Under section 304. when a pv;!.5Ull ii.\u00bb-, st; :ic.t-U  in nt-come an Ljabi|_jtyofexecuto1-\nexecutor of his own wrong, he is answerable to the rightful executor or adminis-- of his own wrong.\ntrator, or to any creditor or lcgatee of the deceased, to the extent of the assets\nwhich may have come to his hands after deducting payments made to the rightful\n\nexecutor or administrator, and payments made in due course of ,administra_tion.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_579\">The section needs no change.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_580\">CHAPTl:ll{ 40\nPOWERS OF AN EXECUTOR OR ADMINISTRATOR\n(SECTIONS 305 TO 315)\n\nI . Preliminary.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_581\">40.1. Sections 305 to 315 deal with powers of an executor or administrator. 59099-\n\nAlthough contained in an enactment dealing with succession, some of the\nsections in this chapter (particularly, section 306 which deal with the survival\nof causes of action) have a much wider interest, and really the &#x27;draftsman, in\ndealing with this topic, has tntereti the realm of torts. It is well-known that the\ntopic Of survival of causes of action has, during the last four or five decades,\nbeen the subject inatter of much legal learning, and of certain legislative develop-\nments in other countries: These legislative developments remedy, to some extent,\ncertain anomalies arising from the earlier common law rule. In India, however,\nthe section. not having been subjected to systematic review in the past, still\nsuffers from a serious drawback. Accordingly, it will be necessary to suggest\nsuitable reforms in this regard. when dealing with the section\n\n40.2. The general principle underlying the provisions in sections 305----315 General principle.\nis that the executor or administrator represents the deceased---~ihough, until he\nobtains probate or letters of administration, no right can be est:,~:~!Eshed in a court\nof justice. This principle is worked out in relation to several matters dealt with\nin this Chapter, such as :---------\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_582\">(a) the. survival of causes of action : sections 305----036.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_583\">&#x27;Sectioii_2l2l and section 0213 (1).\n</p>\n\n\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_584\">2.2 D\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_585\">(b) disposal of property and general powers of the executor or administra-\ntor--sections 307-325.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_586\">The executor or administrator cannot make a profit out of the office.\nAccordingly, sections 309----310 impose certain restrictions based on that prin~\nciple.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_587\">The rest of the sections (31 l----3l5) deal with matters of detail.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_588\">ll. Survival of Causes of Action.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_589\">Section 305---- 40.3. According to section 305, an executor or administrator has the same\n\nIn respect of causes _ _ _ _\no\u00a3 action surviving power to sue in respect of all causes of action that survive the deceased, and may\n\nd\u00b0\u00b0\u00b0&#x27;5\u00b0d and d\u00b0b&#x27;S exercise the same power for the recovery of debts as the deceased had, when\ndue at death. living.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_590\">The section needs no change.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_591\">Section 306. 40.4. Section 306 reads as under :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_363\">&quot;306. All demands whatsoever and all rights to prosecute or &#x27;defend any\naction or special proceeding existing in favour of. or against a person at &#x27;the\ntime of his decease, survive to and against his executors or administrators; except\ncauses of action for defamation, assault, as de\ufb01ned in the Indian Panel Code, or\nother personal injuries not causing the death of the party; and except also cases\nwhere, after the death of the party, the relief sought could not be enjoyed or\ngranting it wouldbe nugatory. _\n\nILLUSTRATIONS\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_364\">(i) A collision takes place on a railway in consequence of some neglect or\ndefault of an official, and a passenger is severely hurt, but not so as to-cause\ndeath. He afterwards dies withogt having brought any action. The cause of\nsection does not survive.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_365\">(ii) A sues for divorce. A dies. The cause of action does not survive to his\nrepresentative.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_592\">The section relates to an important topic whose interest transcends the\nmere limits of &quot;powers of an executor or Admimistijator&quot;. A number of aspects\nwill require examination.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_593\">P\u00b0S&#x27;&quot;\u00b0&quot; 3&quot; K\u00b0&quot;&quot;* 40.5. It should be noted at the outset that in Kerala.&#x27; a State Act contains\nprovisions, inter alia, relating to the survival of causes of action and expressly\nrepeals section 306 by providing that that section, so far as it relates to the\nright of action in torts, shall cease to apply in the State of Kerala. <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_746\">The Act</a>\nsecins to re-enact an earlier Travancore Act&quot; on the subject.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_594\">W583&quot; \u00b0&quot;h\u00a2W1Xim 40.6. Consideration of section 306 of the Central Act may begin with the\nmaxim------personal action dies with the person. Although somewhat obscure in\nits origin, the principle that a personal cause of action dies with the person.\nseems to have been linked with the criminal \ufb02avour of early tort remedies&#x27;\n\nThe maximum was originally introduced to prevent actions of a penal\ncharacter, like trespass and its offshoots, from being brought after the death of the\nwrongdoer against his representatives.&#x27;--&#x27; The main reason was that the trespass was\n&quot;drowned in the felony&quot;. Later, however, the maxim was applied to cases of\ndeath of the injured person, even though the reason&#x27; underlying the maximum\nhad no application.7\n\n&#x27;Ke3rai\u00a71 <a href=\"/doc/31014750/\" id=\"a_747\">Torts (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act, 1976</a> (8 of 1977), /1977) K.L.T. Journal pages\n7- 9.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_595\">9Travancore Law Reform (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act (I 2 of 1124).\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_366\">&quot;Fleming, Torts (1965) page 695.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_367\">&#x27;Holdsworth H.E.L. Vol. 3. page 576.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_368\">5See also Street Torts (1977), page 407.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_369\">&#x27;Death of Wrongdoer in case of [mm] action.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_370\">7 Aydt)lfI&#x27;0&#x27;f~\\&#x27; (&#x27;ummrs. v. s.s. Anicrim, (1917) A.C. as. 43, 44.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_371\">;.&#x27;27\n\nThus, the wide scope that the rule came to acquire was more a product of\nthe accidents of history than of any deliberate view taken as a matter of policy.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_372\">40.7. In England, by the Law Reform (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act, Position in\ufb01ngland\n1934,&#x27; most causes of action survive on the death of a person. The relevant\nprovision of the Act is as follows :--\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_373\">&quot;I. E\ufb02ect of death on certain causes of acziOn :--\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_374\">(1) Subject to the provisions of this section, on the death of any person\nafter the commencement of this Act all causes of action subsisting against\nor vested in him shall survive against or, as the case may be, for the\nbene\ufb01t of, his estate.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_375\">&quot;Provided that this sub-section shall not apply to causes of action for\ndefamation . . . . . . . \n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_376\">There is no exception in England for personal non-fatal injuries. Even as\nregards defamation, there is a section of opinion&quot; that there should be no\nexception.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_377\">40.8. In Australia,&#x27; on the death of any person, all causes of action sub- Position in\nsisting against or vested in him survive against or for the bene\ufb01t of his estate; with &quot;&#x27;&quot;&quot;&quot;&quot;&#x27;a-\nthe exception of causes of action for defamation, seduction, enticement of spouses\nand damage claims for adultery. Even as regards defamation. the cause of action\nsurvives in Tasmania.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_378\">40.9. In Canada, in many States, the law relating to survival of personal Position in Canada\ncauses of action has been reformed by statutes passed by the State Legislatures?\nFor example, under section 38(1), and (2) of the <a href=\"/doc/470004/\" id=\"a_748\">Trustee Act</a> of Ontario,&quot; causes\nof action survive as regards all torts or injuries to the person or to the property\nfor breach of promise of marriage.7 .\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_379\">Substantially to the same effect is the provision in <a href=\"/doc/1249264/\" id=\"a_749\">sections 32</a> and <a href=\"/doc/218073/\" id=\"a_750\">33</a> of the\nTrustee Act of Alberta.&#x27; To the same effect is <a href=\"/doc/334427/\" id=\"a_751\">section 7</a>] of the administration\nAct of British Columbia.&quot; <a href=\"/doc/365950/\" id=\"a_752\">Section 49</a> of the Trustee Act in Manitoba continues\nall actions of tort except action for libel and slander, malicious prosecution. false\nimprisonment or false arrest.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_380\">In New Bruswick, the Survival of Actions Act has followed the <a href=\"/doc/1003813/\" id=\"a_753\">English Act</a>\nof 1934. In Newfoundland, the Trustee Act, <a href=\"/doc/1596418/\" id=\"a_754\">section 22</a>, in effect, provides for\nactions by or against personal representatives of a deceased for wrong to the\nproperty. The Saskatchewan Trustee Act (sections 52 and 53) allows all actions\nto survive for wrongs &quot;not resulting in death, except libel and slander&quot;. In Nova\nScotia, the Survival of Actions Act provides that in the event of death of any\nperson, all causes of action subsisting against or vested in him shall survive.\nexcept actions for adultery and actions for inducing a spouse to leave or remain\napart from him or her spouse.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_381\">40.10. In the USA. in most Sfates statutes have been enacted causing u_s_A.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_382\">tort&quot; actions to survive the death of either party. (In many statutes, exceptions\nare made as to actions for harm peculiarly personal, such as defamation and\nmalicious prosecution). Such statutes are commonly known as &quot;survival Statutes&quot;;\nthey transfer to the estate of the deceased person rights and liabilities which the\ndeceased would had, if he or she had lived.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_383\">&#x27;<a href=\"/doc/678961/\" id=\"a_755\">Section 1(1)</a>. In slightly more than half the States, actions for such torts as defama-\ntion survive?\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_597\">Recommendation . 40.1l.So much as regards the legislative developments elsewhere. We now\nc&#x27;;&#x27;p\ufb01&quot;1o\u00b01:\u00b0&#x27;t?or&quot;;\u00b0ssa&#x27;:l&#x27;j&#x27;t proceed_ to consider the need for reform in the Indian law. ln the \ufb01irst place,\nand pmomj gm-u_we consider the exception in section 306 for assault and other personal injuries\nries not causing to be totally unsound in principle. It is an archaic survival of the rule&#x27; that a\ndeath. personal action dies with the person. The rule itself was unsound,&#x27; being a\nproduct more of historical accident than of any deliberate view of polio . Apart\nfrom this. justice requires that such causes of action should survive. Tbllie mere\naccident of the death of a person ought not to affect the survival of the cause\nof action, whether the person dying is the wronged person or the wrongdoer.\nIn general, tortious liability arises because of certain harm caused by the tort.\nA subsequent event ought not to affect a liability that has already accrued because\n\nof harm already caused.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_598\">We, therefore, recommend deletion of the words &quot;assault . . . . .. or other\npersonal injuries not causing the death of the party&quot; from_ section 306.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_599\">in 0? 40.12. In case, however, our recommendation&#x27; to delete from section 306\nthe exception regarding personal. injury is not accepted, we would like to point\nout the need for a clari\ufb01cation in the light of certain problems raised by the\ncase law on the subject,&#x27; which we proceed to deal with.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_600\">the _ _n\n&quot;personal injury&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_601\">Co It f . p . &#x27; &#x27; h \n,,,e&quot;,;\u00b0, &#x27;,&#x27;,,,,.,-\u00a7\u00a7f,&quot;,,&quot;,&#x27;. 40.13. Case law on section 306 shows that the expression &#x27;personal\n\nprosecution. injuries&quot; in the section has proved to be unfortunate and controversial. For\nexample, the question whether the expression includes malicious prosecution,\nhas been the subject of a conflict of decisions. The majority view on the subject\nwould include malicious prosecution within this expression, the reasoning being\nthat the word &#x27;personal&#x27; in this section must mean all personal injuries in the com-\nmonly accepted use of the word &#x27;personal&#x27;, and cannot be restricted merely to\nphysical injuries to! the person. Hence a suit for malicious prosecution does\nnot, according to the majority view, survive after death. This view has been\ntaken by the Allahabad,&#x27; Bombay,&#x27; Madhya Pra&#x27;desh,\u00b0 Madras,&quot; and Patna&quot; High\nCourts, which hold that a suit for malicious prosecution is a suit for &#x27;personal\ninjury&#x27; within the meaning of section 306.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_602\">&#x27;Tax. Rev. Civ. Stat. Ann. (l955&lt;&gt; \u00bb\\{:t. 5525. Survival of Causes of Action. Reproduced in\nShapiro. Ram Autar Singh, (1919) 52 Indian Cases 348 (Patna).\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_394\">22&#x27;)\n\nThe Madras cases which discuss the matter at some length have speci\ufb01cally\nhold that the words &#x27;personal injuries&#x27; do not mean, bodily injuries only, but\ninclude injuries ejusdem generis with both defamation and assault. and therefore\ninclude injuries such as those by malicious prosecution.&#x27; &#x27;&quot;&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_611\">Relying on earlier cases, it has been held&#x27; in Madras that the maximum\n&#x27;action personalis moritur cum persona&#x27; is a part of the law of this country,\nexcept in so far as it has been modi\ufb01ed by statute, and that in a suit for mali-\ncious arrest, if a defendant dies, the right to sue does not survives&quot;\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_395\">40.14. The Calcutta High Court has, however, held&quot; that malicious pro- C_alcuttaandLahore\n\nsecution is not a &#x27;personal injury&#x27;, and it is not covered by the exception in &quot;&#x27;\u00b0&quot;&#x27;-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_396\">section 306 and, therefore, the right to sue for it survives. This was also the\n\nLahore view,&#x27; which construed the words &#x27;personal injury&#x27; as con\ufb01ned to bodily\n\ninjuries. According to this view, it must be construed with the immediately pre-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_397\">ceding word &#x27;assault&#x27;.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_398\">40.15. This brief resume of the case law on section 306 brings out the Recommendation\nconflict of decisions as to the scope of the expression &quot;personal injuries not &#x27;Q amend_ \u00a7cction\ncausing death&quot;. Whichever be the correct construction of the present wording 3(f5&quot;M&quot;.&quot;\u00b0&#x27;\u00b0&quot;5\nof the section, we are of the view that as a matter of policy, the exception for P \u00b0s\u00b0\u00b0&quot;&quot;\u00b0&quot;&#x27;\n&#x27;personal injuries&#x27; in the context of n,.0n-survival of causes of action (if at all any\nsuch exception is to be retained),&#x27; should be con\ufb01ned to bodily injury, Justice\nrequires that a cause of action for malicious prosecution should survive, There\nare adequate reasons why it should be so. The deceased might, before his death\nhave spent money on defending the prosecution. His estate should, for that\nreason, be allowed to recover the expenses. Apart from this pecuniary aspect, the\nharm caused by a malicious prosecution is of such a character that a remedy\nagainst it should continue to be available even after the death of the person so\nprosecuted. &#x27;&#x27; K&#x27; 5&#x27;&quot;&#x27;&quot;&quot;&quot;  E&#x27;\n\nFor the reasons stated above, we recommend that if the exception for\npersonal injuries not causing death in section 306 is not to be deleted,&#x27; an amend-\nment should be made to section 306 by de\ufb01ning &#x27;personal injury&#x27; as (i) including\nany disease and any impairment of a person&#x27;s physical or mental condition,&quot; and\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_399\">(ii) excluding injury caused by malcious prosecution.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_612\">40.16. We are also of the view, that, on principle, causes of action for Survival of among\ndefamation should survive after death. Whatever be the thinking on the subject for defamation.\nin the West at the time when the Act of 1865 (the predecessor of the <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_759\">Succession\nAct</a> of 1925) was enacted, Indian social notions would rather favour an approach\npermitting survival of such causes of action. Reputation, in the eye of the\nIndian Society, is certainly as precious as property. It may sound odd to quote\nliterary sources in a legal discussion, but the subject--matter is such that it\nwould be proper to draw support from the thinking of great writers. Shakespeare,\nin Othello,&quot; has thus described the value of reputation, in contrast with money :\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_400\">&quot;Good name in man and woman, dear my lord.\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_401\">is the immediate jewel of their souls;\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_402\">Who steals my purse steals trash;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_403\">&#x27;tis something. nothing:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_404\">1Marwadi V. All demands whatsoever and all rights to prosecute or defend any\naction or special proceeding existing in favour of or against a person at the\ntime of his decease, survive to and against his executors or administrators or\nrepresenztiiives, except . . . . . . .- in cases where, after the death of the party,\nthe relief sought could not be enjoyed or granting it would be nugatory.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_620\">ILLUSTRATION\n\n:;=  :3:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_434\">&quot;A sues for divorce. A dies. However, we must deal with a suggestion made by a State Govern- state Government\nment&#x27; for the amendment of the law. The suggestion is to amend section 317(1) <a href=\"/doc/324040/\" id=\"a_792\">10</a> am\u00e9nd 5- 317.\nso as to provide that when letters of administration are granted to the Administra-\ntor-General, it shall not be necessary for him to \ufb01le&quot; the inventory and accounts\nrequired to be \ufb01led by that section.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_679\">41.16. In our view, the suggested amendment has considerable merit, there Merits Of Suggested\n\nbeing good reasons for exempting the Administrator-General from the operation &quot;&quot;&quot;\u00b0&quot;dm\u00b0&quot;&#x27;*\n\nof section 317. In the \ufb01rst place, he is already exempt from the major obligation\n\nto execute an administration bond] and it is logical that he should be exempted\n\nfrom section 317 also. In the second place, his accounts are audited regularly&quot;\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_478\">by the competent officers.&#x27; Hence, no further security is required. In the third\n\nplace, his position, being statutory, is quite different from that of a purely\n\nnon-ol\ufb01cial executor. It would, therefore, be proper to exempt him from the duty\n\nto \ufb01le an inventory of his own accord; though the power of the court to require\n\nhim to do so may be preserved.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_479\">41.17. Sikh or Jain or an exempted person.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_683\">The illustration is as follows :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_505\">ILLUSTRATION\n\n&quot;A dies, having his domicile in a c0unt&#x27;ry where instruments under seal have\n\npriority over instruments not under seal, leaving movable property to the.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_506\">value of 5,000 rupees, and immovable property to the value of 10,000\nrupees, debts on instruments under seal to the amount of 10,000 rupees,\nand debts on instruments not under seal to the same amount. The creditors\nholding instruments under seal receive half of their debts out of the proceeds\nof the moveable estate. The proceeds of the immoveable estate are to be\napplied in payment of the debts on instruments not under seal until one-half\nof such debts has been discharged. This will leave 5,000 rupees which are\nto be distributed rateably amongst all the creditors without distinction. in\nproportion to the amount which may retain due to them.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_63\">239</span>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_507\">41.25. The only part of the section that requires comment in sub--section (2). _CriI71it1aIi0|10fsub-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_684\">whose utility seems rather di\ufb02icult to appreciate. The sub--section assumes Ilia: by 5\u00b0C&quot;\u00b0&quot; (27-\nvirtue&#x27; of sub-secti0n ( l ). the creditor has received some kind of special rig/its.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_685\">This assumption is not, however. correct when one has regard to present sub-\nsection (I ). The situation seems to have arisen by reason of certain accidents\nof drafting. This point will be best understood if the history of the section is\nexamined, Sub-section (l) of section 324 corresponds to section 283 of the &#x27;\nAct of 1865 (10 of I865). Sub-section (2) corresponds to section 284 of that\nAct. Before 1889. section 283 lie. the predecessor of present sub-section (l)\nwas di\ufb02erent from the present rule. In substance. it applied the law of the\ncountry of riomicile. At that time, therefore, the provision contained in section\n284 (which was the predecessor of sub-section (2)] made sense. in l889 (by\nAct 6 of l889). section 283 was amended so as to read (in substance) as\nsection 324 (I) now reads.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_686\">In section &#x27;283 of the <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_796\">Succession Act</a> (10 of 1865). (as originally enacted).\nthe words &quot;the country in which he was domiciled&quot; were used. Subsequently.\nby Act 6 of 1889, the words &quot;British India&quot; were substituted for the words\n&quot;the country in which he was domiciled&quot; and the illustration originally attached\nto section 283 was repealed. This was really a change of suhstztnce. and not a\nmere verbal amendment.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_687\">At the time of the amendment of 1889.3 however. section 284 was\n(apparently by slip) not amended. The un-amended section was carried forward\nin present section 324 (2).\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_688\">41.20. it may be mentioned that section 283 (as originally enacted in Original rule of\n\n1865) followed the English law as regards the priority of debts. as it then En8liShlaW-\nobtained in England.&quot; The vexed question was, what rule was to govern in the\nadministration of assets----the law of the domicile or the law of the situs&#x27;? Sir J.\nRotnilly, in Wilson V. Ltidy Dunsagy,&#x27; held that the personal estate of the tcstator\nmust be administered on the principle of the law of his dtmih\ufb01le. In Cooke V,\nGregson,&quot; it was decided that an lrish iudgment---4:reditor of it testator doimz-[let]\nin Ireland was entitled in England to priority, according to Irish law, in respect\nof assets which had been brought from Ireland to Enland&#x27;. But the observations\nof Kindersley V.C., in that case. although not necessary. appeared to put the\nquestion as though it were rather dependent on the .w&#x27;m.r of .&#x27;/It&#x27; tI.S&#x27;\\&#x27;(&#x27;/\\&quot;&#x27;[l1all on\nthe domicile of the deceased.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_689\">41.27. Section 283. as it found place in the <a href=\"/doc/1450343/\" id=\"a_797\">Indian Succession Act</a>, followed Subscquenz d\u00a2yc-\nthe English law as then understood. Subsequently, in 1885, in England, it was iopmems in Eng.\nlaid&#x27; down&#x27; that if a man domiciled in England dies possessing assets in France, lish law.\nthe French assets must be collected in France and distributed according to\nthe law of France, but that, in the administration of assets in England, a creditor\n(of whatever nationality) is entitled to be paid equally with English creditors\nin the same class. In 1889. the Indian legislature by Act 6 of the 1889 (following\nthe English law as laid down in I885) amended section 283, by replacing the\nwords &#x27;the country in whicli he was domiciled,&quot; by the words &#x27;British India&#x27;.\nit is now well established that administration?-&#x27;&quot; is not governed by the law\nwhich governs succession (last tlomicile)--~but by the law the country where\nadministration takes place.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_690\">4 L28. Section 331 provides that for the purpose of :tlJ&#x27;.ttetttct&#x27;.l. a iegacyscction 331.-A\nfor life. a sum appropriate by the will to produce an annuity. and the value ol |\u00a2gaC&gt;&#x27;l&#x27;0rlI&#x27;fe-\nan annuity when no sum has been appropriated to produce it. shall he treated\nas general legacies.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_706\">The section needs no change.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_707\">CHAPTER 42\nAssuwr TO A LEGACY BY EXECUTOR on ADMINISTRATOR\n&#x27; (SECTIONS 332 to 337;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_708\">42.1. Sections 332 to 337 lay down the law relating to assent to a legacy of Scheme of the sec-\ntions.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_709\">an executor or administrator.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_710\">The general principle is that assent of the executor or administrator is\nnecessary to complete the title of a legatee to his legacy: section 332. This. in\nfact, could be derived from the broader principle that it is the executor or\nadministrator who represents the deceased.&#x27; and in whom &#x27;the title vests until\nsteps are taken to &quot;divest his interest.&quot;-&#x27; Once the assent is given. it takes etfect\nfrom the death of the tcstator; section 336, Payment or tlzrlivcry oi&#x27; the legacy.\nhowever, by the executor is not obligatory. until one year expires after death :\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_521\">sections 337 and 338.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_522\">The rest of the sections regulate certain matters of detail. We now proceed\nto a consideration of each section in detail,\n\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_711\">43.2. Section 332&#x27; reads :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_523\">&quot;332. The assent of the executor or admitlistrator is l&#x27;tCCCs.sttt&#x27;_\\&#x27; to complete\na legzatces title to his legacy.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_524\">ILLUSTRATION\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_525\">(i) A by his will bequeaths to B his Government paper which is in deposit\nwith the Imperial Bank of India.&quot; The Bank has no authority to deliver the &#x27;\nsecurities, nor B a tight to take possession of them_ without the assent of\nthe executor.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_526\">Iii) A by his will has bequeathed to C his house in Calcutta in the tenancy\nof B. C is not entitled to receive the rents without the assent of the execu-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_712\">tor or administrator.&quot; &#x27;\n\n&#x27;Sec sections 305-315. and discussion relating thereto.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_713\">-&#x27;Con1pare section 333 (l t. and <a href=\"/doc/1748310/\" id=\"a_800\">section 21</a> <a href=\"/doc/1066951/\" id=\"a_801\">1</a>.\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_527\">&quot;Illustration ti) may be revised to substitute &quot;State Bank of India&quot; in place of the words\n&quot;imperial Bank of India&quot;. _\n\n\n\nWant of assent --\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_528\">Recommendation.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_529\">Section 333- p _\nAssent to a speeinc\nbequest.\n</blockquote>\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_66\">242</span>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_530\">The precise purport of this section remains obscure, until one turns to the\nremaining sections-----particularly, section 336. Section 336 provides that an assent\nto the title relates back to the date of death.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_531\">Thus, it is not a case of &quot;no title until assent&quot;. but &quot;inchoatc title until\nassent&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_714\">42.3. it has been held by Bombay High Court&#x27; that even where it was\nexpressly directed in the will that the legatee may take possession of his legacy\nwithout the assent of the executor, yet. if he does take p0.s&#x27;sessi&#x27;nz, the executor may\nmaintain an action for trespass.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_715\">The Calcutta High Court has held&#x27; that the legatee has a vested right to\nthe legacy even though the assent of executor is not given. with the result that\nany alienation by the legatee before assent by the executor is not void, but\nmerely inchoatc until assent is given. It was explained that the section is meant to\nprotect the executor. so that he may have money available for the payment of\ndebts.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_716\">These rulings sh()w that there is some obscurity as to the vesting of title,\npending assent.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"RespArg\" id=\"p_717\">It appears to us that such an important matter as the vesting of title should\nnot be left in doubt. The position should be improved by a suitable clari\ufb01cation.\nto the effect that nothing in this section shall be deemed to invalidate a transfer\nof the property (which is the subject-matter of the legacy) by the legatee before\nassent by the executor or administrator. but every such transfer shall be regarded\nas conditional on the assent of the executor or the administrator, as the case\nmay be. We recommend that section 332 should be amended by inserting an\nExplanation on the above lines.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_718\">42.4. Section 333 reads------\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_532\">&quot;333 (i). The assent of the executor or administrator to a speci\ufb01c bequest\nshall be sufficient to divest his interest as executor or administrator therein,\nand to transfer the subject of the bequest of the legatee, unless the nature\nor the circumstances of the property require that it shall be transferred in\na particular way.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_533\">(2) This assent may be verbal, and it may be either express or implied\nfrom the conduct of the executor or administrator.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_719\">ILLUSTRATI ONS\n\n(it A horse is bequeathed. The executor requests the legatee to dispose of\nit, or a third party proposes to purchase the horse from the executor, and\nhe directs him to apply to the legatee. Assent to the legacy is implied.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_720\">(ii) The interest of a fund is directed by the will to be applied for the\nmaintenance of the legatee during his minority. The executor commences\nso to apply it. This is an assent to the whole of the bequest.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_721\">(iii) A bequest is made of a fund to A and after him to B. The executor\npays the interest of the fund to A. This is an implied assent to the bequest\nto B.\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_722\">(iv) Executors die after paying: all the debts of the testator. but before\nsatisfaction of speci\ufb01c legacies. Assent to the legacies may be presumed.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_723\">(v) A person to whom a specific article has been bequeathed takes posses-\nsion of it and retains it without any objection on the part of the executor.\nHis assent may be presumed.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_724\">The section has created no problems and needs no change.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_725\">&#x27;Harm:/i \\. Pupallal, (1912) I4 Bom. L.R. 732; l.L.R. 37 Bom. 2ll.\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_534\">&quot;fjliagendrii V. Khutranatli. Sections 349 to 355 contain provisions relating to the produce and\n\ninterest of legacies.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_778\">The provisions in sections 349 to 355. though apparently dealing with\n\nmatters of detail. are based on certain broad principles. namely. that 2-~\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_779\">(i) The legatee&#x27;s title (subject to the provisions as to assent of the executor\nor administrator) is e\ufb01eetive from the date of the testators death, so\nthat the &quot;clear produce&quot; of the legacy belongs to him from the date\nof such death; sections 349-350.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_780\">(ii) the right to demand payment of the legacy, however, does not ordinarily\narise unless one year has expired since such death---a principle that\nexplains the provisions (section 351. main paragraph and section 354)\nas to commencement of interest:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_560\">(iii) in certain exceptional cases, however, interest may start running\nbefore the expiry of one year : Section 349, exception, section 352 and\nsection 355. These exceptional cases are based on the existence of\ncircumstances from which a contrary intention could be reasonably\ninferred.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_781\">&#x27;l he rate of interest is dealt with in section 353.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_782\">45.3. Section 349 reads as under :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_561\">&quot;349. The legatee of a speci\ufb01c legacy is entitled to the clear produce thereof.\nit any_ from the testator&#x27;s death. &#x27;\n\nIixccption A specific bequest, contingent in its terms. does not comprise\nthe produce of the legacy between the death of the testator and the vesting&quot;\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_783\">of the legacy. The clear produce of it forms part of the residue of the\ntestator&#x27;s estate.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_784\">. ILLUSTRATION\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_785\">(i) A bequeaths his \ufb02ock of sheep to B. Between the death of A and\ndelivery by his executor the sheep are shorn or some of the ewes produce\nlambs. The wool and lambs are the property of B.\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_786\">(ii) A bqueaths his Government securities to B, but postpones delivery of\nthem till the death of C. The interest which falls due between the death of\nA and the death of C belongs to B, and must, unless he is minor, be paid\nto him as it is received. &#x27;\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_787\">(iii) The testator bequeaths all his four per cent, (&#x27;uoxernment promissory\nnotes to A when he shall complete the age of l8. A. if he completes that\nage, is entitled to receive the notes, but the interest which accrues in respect\nof them between the testator&#x27;s death and A&#x27;s completing l8. forms part of\nthe residue&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_788\">The section needs no change.\n</p>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_68\">247</span>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_789\">45.3. By way of anticipating certain possible objections to the proposed\nextension of the scope of section 367. we may state that the provisions of the\nsection, even after the proposed amendment, would be con\ufb01ned to a person\nnot domiciled in India. Secondly, the provision, in so far as its scope is to be\nextended, will apply only subject to the permission of the Court. The Court, in\ngranting permission, will certainly satisfy itself that the transfer of assets to&#x27; the\nforeign executor or administrator under the section would be in the interests\nof justice. It is better to keep the matter elastic as above, rather than tie down\nthe hands of the Indian executor or administrator or fetter the discretions of the\nCourt by insisting upon the requirement of reciprocity or other rigid criteria.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_847\">CHAPTER 47\nLIABILITY OF AN EXECUTOR OR ADMINISTRATOR FOR DEVASTA}\nTION: SECTIONS 368 AND 369\n\n47.1. Liability of an executor or administrator for devastation is dealt with\nin sections 368 and 369, which needs no introductory comments.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_848\">47.2. Section 368 reads:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_601\">&quot;368. When an executor or administrator misapplies the estate of the\ndeceased, or subjects it to loss or damage, he is liable to make good the loss\nor damage so occasioned.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_602\">ILLUST RATIONS\n\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_603\">(i) The executor pays out of the estate an unfounded claim. He is liable\nto make good the loss.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_604\">(ii) The deceased had a valuable lease renewable by notice which the\nexecutor neglects to give at the proper time. The executor is liable to make\ngood the loss.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_605\">(iii) The deceased had a lease of less value than the rent payable for it,\nbut terminable on notice at a particular time. The executor neglects to give\nthe notice. He is liable to make good the loss&quot;.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_849\">The section needs no change. not having raised any serious difficulties.\n47.3. Section 369 reads--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_606\">&quot;369. When an executor or administrator occasions a loss to the estate by\nneglecting to get in any part of the property of the deceased, he is liable&#x27;\nto make good the amount. .\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_75\">253</span>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_607\">ILLUSTRATIONS\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_608\">(i) The executor absolutely releases a debt due to the deceased from a\n\nsolvent person. or compounds with a debtor who is able to pay in full, The\nexecutor is liable to make good the amount.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_609\">(ii) The executor neglects to sue for a debt till the debtor is able to plead\nthat the claim is barred by limitation and the debt is thereby lost to the\nestate. The executor is liable to make good the amount.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_850\">This section also needs no challgg;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_851\">CHAPTER 48.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_852\">SUCCESSION CERTIFICATES (SECTIONS 370 T0 390)\nl. Prelinzinary\n\n48.1. Sections 37()--39U deal with succession certi\ufb01cates. Under section\n370, a succession certi\ufb01cate cannot be granted in respect of any debt or security\nto which a right is, by section 212 or 213. required to be established by letters\nof administration or probate. There is an exception provided in the case of a\ndeceased Indian Christian.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_853\">The pmeedure for making an application for a succession certi1icate._ and the\nCourt to which it should be made, are dealt with in sections 37] to 373 and\n(where applicable), in section 388. The contents of the certi\ufb01cate are provided\nfor in secton 374. Under section 375. the Judge may require security from the\ngrantee of the certi\ufb01cate.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_854\">. The form of the certi\ufb01cate and &quot;extensions&quot; thereof are regulated by sec-\ntions 376 to 377&#x27;. Amendment of the certi\ufb01cate in respect of powers of the\n\nholder as to securities is provided for in section 378. Fees on certi\ufb01cates are to\n\nbe paid as <a href=\"/doc/161498179/\" id=\"a_804\">laid down in</a> section 379.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_855\">The extent and etfect ol certilicates are dealt with in sections 380--382\nand 387. Section 370 reads----\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_610\">&quot;37O (1) A succession certificate (hereinafter in this Part referred to as a\nCerti\ufb01cate) shall not be granted under this Part with respect to any debt\nor security-to which a right is required by section 212 or section 213 to\nbe established by letters of administration or probate:\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_611\">Provided that nothing contained in this section shall be deemed to prevent\nthe grant of a certi\ufb01cate to any person elziiming: to be entitled to the e\ufb02ects\nof a deceased Indian Christian. or to any part thereof. with respect to any\ndebt or security. by reason that a right thereto can be established by letters\nof administration under this Act.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_612\">&#x27;See Eighth Schedule.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_613\">Sections 370-390 -7\nScheme.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_614\">Section 370. Sub-section (2). clause (by) of section 370 seems to be obsolete and\n(&#x27;;)(t&quot;&#x27;)&#x27; 5\u00b0&quot;&quot;\u00b0&quot; 37\u00b0should be deleted. We recommend accordingly.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_862\">Need for change of 48.4. Section 370 bars the grant of a succession certi\ufb01cate, inter alia, in\n5&quot;b9&quot;&quot;&#x27;\u00b0\u20ac~ cases where letters of administration (or probate) are mandatory, Letters of\nadministration are mandatory in the case of Christians other than Indian Chris-\ntians dying intestate&#x27;-*to mention the most usual situation. We are of the view\nthat this restriction is not required except where probate is mandatory. The bar\nin section 370 should be limited only to cases where probate is mandatory. In the\ncase of probate, the will has to be proved and it is understandable that without\nproof of the will, payment should not be made on the strength of a mere succes-\nsion certi\ufb01cate. But this reasoning does not apply with the same forceto letters\nof administration, where title is sought to be derived on the basis of a right of\ninheritance conferred by law and no formal writing is to be proved in general.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_863\">Recommendation ,- 48.5. Accordingly, we recommend that section 370(1) should be revised\nas to section 370(1) as under ._\n\n( l) A succession Certi\ufb01cate (hereinafter in this Part referred to as a certi-\nficate) shall not be granted under this Part with respect to any debt or\nsecurity to which a right is required by section 212 or section 213 to be\nestablished by . . . . .. probate. (Existing Proviso to be omitted as a con-\nsequential change). &#x27;\n\nSurvivorship under 48.6. We are also of the view that there should be a provision for the\nSection 370- grant of certi\ufb01cate on survivorship.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_864\">By making this amendment, the legislature would be merely giving recogni-\ntion to what is already the practice!-3 in some parts of India.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_865\">48.7. In regard to section 371, however, some obscurity has arisen\nbecause the words used in the section are----&quot;if at that time he had no \ufb01xed place\nof residence,&quot; without indicating whether the \ufb01xed place of residence shoyldvbe\nin India or may be aI1y._where in the world.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_631\">Judicial decisions reveal a certain amount of conllict. Procedure.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_870\">48.21. This takes us to section 372, which reads as under 2-\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_652\">&quot;372.(l) Application for such a certi\ufb01cate shall be made to the District i\u00b0\u00a7:,&#x27;1&#x27;i&#x27;c&#x27;]a,3ig,--f;,r\n\nJudge by petition signed and veri\ufb01ed by or on behalf of the applicant incerti\ufb01cate.\nthe manner prescribed by <a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_812\">the Code of Civil Procedure</a>, for the signing and 5 of 1908.\nver\ufb01cation of a plaint by or on behalf of a plaintiff, and setting forth the\nfollowing particulars. namely :--\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_653\">(a) the time of the death of the deceased;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_654\">(b) the ordinary residence of the deceased at the time of his death\nand, if such residence was not within the local limits of the jurisdiction\nof the Judge to whom the application is made, then the property of\nthe deceased within those limits;\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_655\">(c) the family or other near relatives of the deceased and their res-\n</blockquote><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_871\">pective residences;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_872\">(d) the right in which the petitioner claims;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_873\">(e) the absence of any impediment under section 370 or under any\nother provision of this Act or any other enactment, to the grant of the\ncerti\ufb01cate or to the validity there of if it were granted; With reference to this section (section 372), there seems to be a\nconflict of decisions on the question whether, the word &quot;debt&quot; includes movable Mganing of subt-\nproperty that has been pledged, In a Bombay case2, the question arose whether in section 372.\na succession certi\ufb01cate could be granted in respect of gold or ornaments pledged\nby the deceased with a bank. It was held that the pledgee&#x27;s obligation to return\nto the pledger&#x27;s heirs the pledged movable property was not a &#x27;debt&#x27;, as it\nwas not a speci\ufb01c. or ascertained or liquidated sum of money, A similar View\n\nhad been taken in a <a href=\"/doc/1378256/\" id=\"a_814\">Patna</a> case?</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_877\">However, an Allahabad judgment* takes a different view in this regard.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_878\">We have discussed the matter under section 214&#x27;, and need not repeat here\nthe points made in that discussion.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_879\">&#x27;Para 48.16. supra.\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_880\">&#x27;lkanchhoddas Govinddas Bmzatwala, (1976) 78 Born. In this respect, sections 223 and 236.\nwhich relate to the grant of probate and letters of administraion respectively.\nmay be contrasted. These sections prohibit grant to a minor. The rcilson why the\ntwo sets of provisions are differently frtimed appears to be this. Probate is granted\nto an executor appointed by the tcstator. Normally, the executor would not be a\nniiiior. but in the rare case in which he is a minor. the prohibition against grant\nof probate~ai1d consequential refusal by the court to grant the probate to him--\nwould not cause serious hardship. because letters of administration with the will\nannexed can still be obtained by another suitable person,\n\nIt may be noted that the law does not impose very rigid limitations as to\nthe grant of letters of administration. in the case of a succcssioii certi\ufb01cate. how-\never. the position is ditlcrent. Only the person who has the right to the debt\nin qucstioii can apply. so that the \ufb01eld of choice is limited and is determined, not\nby statutory provisions or by the tcstator&#x27;s directions or by the orders of the\ncourt, but by external circuinstances~--tlie person who have survived the deceased\nand stand in the prescribed relationship to him. Apparently for this reason, the\nAct does not provide a total bar as such against the minor being grained succession\ncerti\ufb01cate. Under section 372(] ) (d), the right which the petitioner claims has to be\nspeci\ufb01ed in the petion, this indicating that the claimant must be a person who has\nsome title or interest in the debt. If the person who has some title or interest\nhappens to be a minor. only he can apply for a succession certi\ufb01cate.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_891\">48.25. The question that should really survive for practical consideration is\nonly one as to the procedure to be followed by the minor. The grant of succes-\nsion certi\ufb01cate to a minor as such cannot be denied as a matter or law. but\nsafeguards could still be laid down. Of course, the saleguards should not impose\nvery severe restrictions on the pursuit of the remedy for enforcing a right that\nis already vested in the minor.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_892\">Hzlviltg J&#x27;cgLii&#x27;d to the abmc coiisi\u00ab.let&#x27;;itioiis. the proper course, in our view.\nwould be to provide---by way of clari\ufb01cation and by way of indicating the correct\nprocedure--that where the applicant for succession certi\ufb01cate is a minor, he may\napply through a next friend as if he were a pltiiiiti\ufb01 in a suit and the provisions\n<a href=\"/doc/161831507/\" id=\"a_816\">of the Code of Civil Procedure</a>. 1908 should. so far as may be. apply in relation\nto such l&#x27;iC.&#x27;\\[ trieiids as they apply in relation to a next friend suing under im-\nCode&#x27;-&#x27;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_893\">We may add that the provision proposed would not only possess practical\nutility. but would also be consistent with the object underlying the provisions\nof the Act as to succession certi\ufb01cate. It is the stage before passing of the order for grant of stage of refund.\n2: certi\ufb01cate, could it be any later stage&#x27;? The nature of the controversy Wm be\napparent on a consideration of selected cases on the seCtion_\n\n48.35. lll a Madras case&quot;. the District Judge. Tihnevelly, referred the follow- The case law.\ning question to the High C()lall 1\u00bb-\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_682\">&quot;Up to what stage in the proceeding for succession certi\ufb01cate is an applicant\nentitled to obtain a refund 7&quot; &#x27;\n\n&#x27;S\u00a2mkm&#x27;o ,-lyyar v. i\\&quot;ai&#x27;Itai&#x27; 3&#x27;/10()[V)p\ufb02IltlI&#x27;, ( l 898)\nl.L.R. 21 Mad. 241.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_85\">262</span>\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_683\">\\\n\nThe High Court held that. if the tip/ilication is allowed, i.c.. if the order\nfor the grant of a certi\ufb01cate has been made, the sum in deposit becomes at once\nlegally appropriated, as duty, to the extent of the debt covered by the order, and\ncannot be refunded. in other cases, we think that refund can be made.&quot; This case\nwas decided under section 14 of the Succession Certi\ufb01cate Act_ 1889. to which\nsection 379 of the present Act corresponds.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_684\">48.30, A Nagpur case&#x27;, however, seems to have taken a di\ufb02erent view. The\napplicant applied for a succession certi\ufb01cate and deposited the required fee. The\n(&#x27;ourt allowed the t7[)[)liC&#x27;(1ti0t1 for succession certi\ufb01cate subject to the appli-\ncant&#x27;s furnishing security, but the applicant failed to furnish it. Thereafter, the\napplicant applied for a refund of the sum deposited by him. The lower appellate\ncourt, relying on the Madras case mentioned above? rejected the application. The\nHigh Court, in revision, held that section 379 (3) lays down two conditions\n</blockquote><blockquote id=\"blockquote_685\">(i) the sum should be received under sub-section (1), and (ii) it should not\nbe expended under sub--section (2), If these two conditions are satis\ufb01ed, the sum\nshould be refunded. Since in this case both the conditions were satis\ufb01ed, the High\nCourt ordered a refund.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_920\">The same view has been subsequently taken in it Lahore case&quot;.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_921\">Comment on case 48.37. it is, with respect, suggested that on the language of sub-section\n\nlaw. (2&#x27;), refund is not permissible Once the application is allowed. Under that sub-\nsection the money can be spent only for purchase of the stamp. it thus impliedly\nbars refund, once the application is allowed,\n\nThe opening words of section 379(2) are &quot;if the application is allowed&quot;,\nand not &quot;when the certif\ufb01cate. is granted&quot;. Therefore, once a court has allowed an\napplication for succession certi\ufb01cate and has passed an order to that effect.\nwhether with or without a condition, the sum can be appropriated only towards the\npayment of the stamp. If the applicant has failed to ful\ufb01l a condition, the State\ncannot be deprived of its legitimate ducs_ more particularly when the court has\nheard the parties, spent its time and exercised its mind on the problems.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_922\">Recommendation 48.38. We recommend that in order to make the position clear, it new sub-\nto &#x27;insert Sl_1l&gt;- section should be inserted in section 37&#x27;), as follows :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_686\">sect1on(4)tn section &#x27;\n\n379 and *0 am\u00b0&quot;d- &quot;(4). Notwithstanding anything in Bombay Regulation No, VIII of 1827,\nthe provisions of section 370, sub-section (2), section 372, sub-section (1),\nclause (f) and sections 374. 375, 376, 377, 378, 379, 381, 382, 384, 387, 388\nand 389 with respect to certi\ufb01cates under this Part and applications there-\nfor, and of section 317 with respect to the exhibition of inventories and\naccounts by e.\\ecutors and administrators, shall, so far as they can be made\napplicable, apply, respectively,&#x27; to certi\ufb01cates granted under that Regula-\ntion, and applications made for certi\ufb01cates thereunder. after the 1st day\nof May I889, and to the exhibition of inventories and accounts by the\nholders of \\uch certificates so granted.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_954\">This section also needs no change.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_955\">CHAPTER 49\n\nSUCCESSION BY HOMICIDE\n\nSECTION 390A\n( Proposed )\n\nI, Introductory\n\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_956\">49.l. We are concerned in this Chapter with a fundamental principle of\n\npublic policy, namely, that a man should not be allowed to derive a bene\ufb01t\n\n&#x27;Information as to empowerment is being collected.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_957\">Section 389----~sur-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_958\">render ofsupersedcd\nand invalid certi\ufb01-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_959\">cates.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_960\">Section 390-Provi-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_961\">sions with respect to\ncerti\ufb01cate under\nBombay Regula-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_962\">tion VIII of 1837.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_963\">Scope of the Chap-\n</p><p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_964\">ter.\n</p>\n\n\n<span class=\"hidden_text\" id=\"span_89\">266</span>\n\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_965\">resulting from his own crime. lhis principle, well-recognised in the legal systems\nof many countries.&quot; including India,&quot; has not yet been expressly enacted in the\n<a href=\"/doc/1450343/\" id=\"a_826\">Indian Succession Act</a>. The object of this Chapter is to consider the need for\ncodi\ufb01cation of the above principle in it suitable form in the Act. after a brief\nexamination of the present position in this regard.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_966\">The highe\ufb02a&quot; 49.2. All laws must conform to a higher law. An American case wliiclt is\na good example of this approach is Riggs v. Palmer, where the New York Court\nOf Appeals held that 2: legatee did not gain title to property which\nhe would have received as the result of murdering his testator. liar] J. said that\nthere are certain principles that &quot;have their foundatt&quot;on in universal law admini--\nstered in all civilised countries&quot; and that &quot;all laws, as well as all contracts, may\nbe controlled in their operation and ellcct by general. fundamental maxims of\nthe common law&quot;. t\n\nMotive immatet-iaJ_ -19.3. Accordingly. where a person murders another he generally should\nnot be allowed to enjoy the property he acquires as the result of that death.\n</p><blockquote id=\"blockquote_719\">&quot;A man shall not slay his benefactor and thereby take his bounty?&quot; It is not\nconsidered relevant what the murderers&#x27; motive for the murder was.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_967\">/P\u00a3:\u00b0;&quot;\u00b0&quot; &quot;me&quot; &#x27;he 49.4. .--\\t present. lio\\\\e\\&#x27;cr. there is no express provision in the Indian\n&#x27; <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_827\">Succession Act</a> debarring a person who has committed homicide for his right\nto succeed to the estate of the person whose death he has caused. No doubt, this\nwould be the position under the rules of the common law (to be dealt with\nlater)? But it is appropriate that this important topic should be dealt with by\na speci\ufb01c statutory provision in an Act which is a consolidating enactment.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_968\">In order to obtain a proper perspective of the matter. it would be useful\nfirst to deal brie\ufb02y with the position in cases not governed by the Act, and\nthen to recommend the amtndment that could be appropriately made in the\nAct.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_969\">ll. Hindu Law and Muslim_law\n\n1-[indu&#x27;La\\v. 49.5. According to Hindu law. even before the passing of the <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_828\">Hindu\nSuccession Act</a>} no heirship to another person could be claimed by or through\none who has been a privy to the murder of that person. This rule was one of\npublic policy and had been given effect to by the Privy Council. &quot;--&#x27;\u00b0 relying on\nancient texts. Even before the Privy Council decision. substantially the same\nresult had been reached by the High Courts in India.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_970\">A Madras case may be cited&quot; in illustration. Defendant. the mother of \nhad been charged, along with another accused, with having murdered S. Defen-\ndant was acquitted, though the other accused was convicted. Plainti\ufb01, as the\nperson next in succession to S. (after the defendant). now sued for a declaration\nof his right to the property of S on the ground that the defendant was not entitled\nto the property, inasmuch as she had. ( as the plaintiff alleged) been party to the\nmurder. The subordinate iudge dismissed the suit without trying the question\nwhether he defendant had been a party to the murder. On appeal it was held by\nthe High Court that the question should have been tried. The reasoning was\nas follows:\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_720\">&#x27;Clearer \\. .\\-lut1tu/ Rt&#x27;.\\&#x27;(&#x27;l&#x27;\\&#x27;t&#x27; l*m1\u00ab/ lv~&#x27;\\0(&#x27;I&#x27;(/IIIOII, (1982) l QB. If the defendant was a party to the murder, her\nwrongful act, while not preventing the vesting in her of the inheritance, disentitled\nher to any bene\ufb01cial interest in it. Such bene\ufb01cial interest would vest in those\nwho would be entitled to it if the guilty heir were out of the way.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_728\">49.6. T he <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_831\">Hindu Succession Act</a>* now provides as follows;--- Provision in Hindu\n&quot; _ _ &#x27; <a href=\"/doc/685111/\" id=\"a_832\">Succession Act</a>.\n</p><p data-structure=\"CDiscource\" id=\"p_972\">25. Common Law u,,,,,&#x27;,,.,,, law\n\n49.9. There is enough authorityin common law for such a bar. In England,\nas early as 1892,5 it was laid down that murder forfeits all bene\ufb01ts under the\nwill of his victim. In 1914, this principle was extended from murder to man-\nslaughter.&#x27; In regard to intestacy, it was decided in 1935 that the same rule of\npublic policy applies.&#x27;\n\n49.10. The general rule* in England is that a person may not derive a\nbene\ufb01t resulting from his own crime.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_975\">the principle that no criminal can retain a bene\ufb01t which acrues to him\nfrom crime&quot; is now well accepted.&#x27;&#x27;-&#x27;&#x27;\n\nThe English cases all deal with direct inheritance of the murderer from the\nvictim. The problem of indirect inheritance does not seem to have arisen in\n&quot;England.&quot;\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Section\" id=\"p_976\">49.11. The deceased had made a will in favour of his wife. &#x27;\n\n49.19. But the decision referred to above&#x27; has not escaped criticism, Golf\nand Jones state their comments in this respect as follows&quot; :----\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_744\">&quot;lt is to be hoped that these views will not prevail. Fears that the relaxa-\ntion of Fry. L.J.&#x27;s principle would be &quot;harmful and dangerous&quot; are, in\nour opinion, misconcieved. There is much to be said for the view that the\nprinciple should have no application if the criminal has no men: rm, but is\nnevertheless found guilty.&quot; A\n\n&quot;it is hardly defensible that a person who is &#x27;guilty but insane&#x27; can take\nit bene\ufb01t under his victims will, but a person who is guilty by reason of\ndiminished responsibility cannot.&quot;\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_996\">&#x27;Para 49. l.. mpru.\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Conclusion\" id=\"p_997\">&#x27;Para 49.4. .\\&#x27;I1[1I&#x27;(l.\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_745\">&quot;Para 49. 8. sup/11.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_746\">&#x27;Goff and Jones. The\nmost that can be expected of the courts is to limit the rule to murder and\nmanslaughter, and even to exclude manslaughter based on negligence would\nnow call for much judicial valour. Beyond this. it seems necessary to look to\nParliament.&quot; &#x27;\n\n49.20. In India, the legislature has not had an opportunity of examining\nthe pros and cons of the matter. Having considered the position carefully, we are\nof the View that the proposed disability in regard to succession should apply to\ncases of culpable homicide (whether or not amounting to murder), but should\nnot extend to cases showing a lesser degree of mens rea-----e.g. causing death by\nrash or negligent act?\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_756\">pro\ufb01t by his own\nin\nthe\n\nPublic polic_\\-----and the principle that no man should\n&quot;wrong&quot;----do not seem to necessitate the imposition of a disquali\ufb01cation\ncases of rashness or negligence. It is true that for reasons of deterrence,\n\ncriminal law in most countries punishes rash or negligent conduct causing death ,\n\n(Or certain other civil consequences). But such conduct does not attract the\ndoctrine that no man should pro\ufb01t by his own &quot;wrong&quot;. The emphasis that the\ndoctrine places on &quot;wrong&quot; would seem to indicate that its demands would be\namply met by con\ufb01ning the disability to cases where the mental element is of the\nquality described in <a href=\"/doc/305371/\" id=\"a_842\">section 299</a> of the Indian Penal Code.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_757\">49.21. In particular, the offence under <a href=\"/doc/1371604/\" id=\"a_843\">section 304A</a>, I.P.C. (causing death\nby rash or negligent act) is of in\ufb01nite variety. It may cover even a situation\nwhere the morally culpable glement is minimal. Serious cases of rashness would\nreally fall under section 304 (read with section 299) of the <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_844\">Indian Penal Code</a>.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_758\">Extreme cases of rashness would fall even under section 302 (read with\nsection 300, fourth clause). These two sections, read together, cover a really\n&quot;culpable homicide&quot;-----.culpable in the moral sense. Only cases with a. lesser\n&#x27;degree of mental element would fall within section 304A. It is on this rationable\nthat there is justi\ufb01cation for not attaching a disquali\ufb01cation (in regard to succes-\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_759\">sion) to mere rashness or negligence which does not indicate aiquality of mens,\n\nrea covered by <a href=\"/doc/305371/\" id=\"a_845\">sections 299</a> and <a href=\"/doc/626019/\" id=\"a_846\">300</a> of the Indian Penal Code.\n</blockquote>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_998\">49.22. Some illustrative cases under section 304A of the Penal Code may\nbe referred to in this context. In one Allahabad case} the accused (a woman)\nreceived a powder from an enemy of her relative, took no precaution to ascer-\ntain whether it was noxious and mixed it with the relative&#x27;s food, believing that\nby so doing she would become rich. It was held that her conduct was wanting\nin that prudence and circumspection which every human being is supposed to\nexercise, and as, by her rash and thoughtless act, she had caused death, she was\nguilty of an offence under section 304A. <a href=\"/doc/1569253/\" id=\"a_847\">I.P.C</a>. &#x27;\n\n<span class=\"citetext\" data-sentiment=\"Pos\" data-docid=\"773482\" id=\"span_96\">In a <a href=\"/doc/773482/\" id=\"a_848\">Calcutta</a> case,&#x27; the accused operated on another person for internal\npiles by cutting them out with an ordinary knife. The man died from haemor-\nrhagc. The Court held that he had no intention to casue the death of the patient.\nIn the circumstances of the case. it was held that the conviction under section\n304A was a proper one.</span>\n</p>\n<p data-structure=\"Precedent\" id=\"p_999\">In another case.5 the facts were as follows :--\n</p>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_760\">The factory manufacturin_:: \ufb01reworks etc. was situated in close proximity\nto residential quarters. An explosion in the factory resulted in injuries to, and\ndeath of, some persons. The explosives were of highly hazardous and dangerous\nnature and their possession was prohibited. They were stored in the premises at\nthe time of the occurrence. It was held by the Supreme Court that the appeal-\nlants who were licence holders for manufacturing explosives in the factory were\n\n&#x27;Gareth Miller. I968 S.C. 1319.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_764\">425, 423.\n</blockquote>\n\n\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_765\">&#x27;71\n\nI\n\nliable to be convicted under sections 304A and 337, although there was no\ndirect evidence of the immediate cause of the explosion. &quot;The manufacturers un-\ndoubtedly displayed a high degree of negligence by allowing or causing to be\nllscd explosives of sensitive compositions and substances in the manufacturing\nof t&quot;ireworks. \\&#x27;.&#x27;lllCl&#x27;. must be the et\ufb01cient cause of the explosion.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_766\">In yet another case} the accused, knowing that a pistol was loaded, was\ntrying to unload it and while doing so, acted so negligently that the pistol went\noff and, as a result the complainant&#x27;s son was killed. It was held that the accused\nwas guilty under section 304A, &#x27;[.P.C.\n\nIn a Bombay case} the accused was out shooting with the &#x27;deceased in the\njungle. While separate from the deceased, the accused saw something moving\nin the jungle. Without waiting to see what it was, the accused \ufb01red and shot the\ndeceased. It was held that the case fell within section 304, l.P.C.\n\nWe are citing cases to show the wide scope of section 304A.\n</blockquote>\n<blockquote id=\"blockquote_767\">V&#x27; l.
